nodes.definition_en: The total money a company takes in from selling its products or services over a period, before any costs are subtracted. It sits at the top of the income statement, which is why it's called the 'top line'.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司在一段时期内销售产品或服务所收到的全部金额,尚未扣除任何成本。它位于利润表最上方,因此被称为「第一行」(top line)。

nodes.definition_en: The direct costs of producing the goods or services a company sold — mainly materials and production labor. Subtracting it from revenue gives gross profit.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司生产已售出商品或服务所发生的直接成本,主要是原材料和生产人工。营业收入减去它就得到毛利润。

nodes.definition_en: What's left of revenue after subtracting the direct cost of making the product. Divided by revenue, it becomes the gross margin (毛利率), a basic read on how profitable each sale is.
nodes.definition_zh: 营业收入扣除产品直接生产成本后剩下的部分。除以营业收入即为毛利率,用来初步判断每一笔销售有多赚钱。

nodes.definition_en: Profit from a company's core business operations, before interest and taxes.
nodes.definition_zh: 企业主营业务经营所得的利润,扣除利息和所得税之前。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Under US GAAP, operating income excludes investment gains and fair-value changes. Under Chinese Accounting Standards, 营业利润 includes them, so a Chinese figure is usually higher and isn't directly comparable to the US 'operating income' line.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国准则下 operating income 不含投资收益与公允价值变动损益;而 CAS 的营业利润将其计入,故口径更宽,通常高于美式 operating income,两者不可直接对比。

nodes.definition_en: The profit a company has left after every expense — costs, interest, and taxes — is subtracted from revenue. It sits at the foot of the income statement, hence the 'bottom line'.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司在从营业收入中扣除所有支出(成本、利息、税费)之后剩下的利润。它位于利润表最下方,因此被称为「最后一行」(bottom line)。

aliases_zh: 归属于母公司股东的净利润
nodes.definition_en: The slice of a company's net profit that belongs to the parent company's own shareholders, after setting aside the part owned by minority (non-controlling) investors in its subsidiaries.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司净利润中归属于母公司股东的那部分,即从合并净利润里扣除子公司中少数股东(非控股股东)所占份额之后剩下的利润。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Both accounting systems compute this line, but they differ on which number is treated as 'the' profit figure. In China, 归母净利润 is the headline number that media and investors quote, and it is deliberately not the same as total net profit — the difference is minority interest. In US reporting, the number people usually quote as 'net income' is often the whole consolidated total, so lining up a Chinese 归母 figure against a US 'net income' can mismatch unless you check which basis each one is on.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两套准则都会计算这一行,但「哪个数字代表利润」的习惯不同。在A股,归母净利润是媒体和投资者引用的头条数字,且刻意区别于净利润总额,差额即少数股东损益。而美股常被当作「net income」引用的往往是合并总额,因此把中国的归母数据与美式 net income 直接对齐时,需先确认各自口径,否则容易错配。

aliases_zh: 扣除非经常性损益后的净利润
nodes.definition_en: A company's net profit with one-off, unusual items stripped out — such as asset sales, subsidies, or investment windfalls — to show the profit its core business actually earns.
nodes.definition_zh: 把一次性、非经常的项目(如资产处置、政府补贴、投资意外收益)从净利润中剔除后的利润,用来反映主营业务真正赚到的钱。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Both markets adjust reported profit, but in opposite directions. In China, 扣非净利润 is a regulator-defined 'core' profit that removes one-off items to give a cleaner, usually lower figure. US non-GAAP earnings are company-defined and typically add items back (like stock-based compensation), giving a higher figure. So a Chinese 'adjusted profit' is stricter than the headline number, while a US 'adjusted' one is usually more flattering.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两地都会对报告利润做调整,但方向相反。中国的扣非净利润由监管统一定义,剔除一次性项目,得到更「干净」、通常更低的核心利润。美股的 non-GAAP 由公司自定,往往是把项目(如股份支付)加回去,得到更高的利润。因此中式「调整后利润」比头条数字更严,美式「调整后」通常更宽松。

nodes.definition_en: The part of a subsidiary that a parent company controls but doesn't fully own — the stake held by other, minority shareholders. It shows up on the balance sheet as equity and on the income statement as the slice of profit belonging to those outside investors.
nodes.definition_zh: 母公司虽然控股、但并非百分之百持有的子公司中,由其他少数股东所占的那部分。它在资产负债表上体现为权益(少数股东权益),在利润表上体现为归属这些外部股东的那部分利润(少数股东损益)。

aliases_en: Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization
nodes.definition_en: A measure of profit that adds back interest, taxes, and non-cash charges (depreciation and amortization) to net income, meant to approximate the cash a business's operations throw off before financing and accounting effects.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种利润口径,在净利润基础上加回利息、税费以及折旧摊销等非现金项目,用来近似衡量经营活动在剔除融资与会计影响前产生的现金。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The definition is the same in both markets, but availability differs. US companies routinely present EBITDA. A-share financial statements usually don't break it out as a line, so analysts reconstruct it from the reported numbers — and because people make slightly different choices about what to add back, two calculated EBITDA figures for the same Chinese firm may not match.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两地对 EBITDA 的定义相同,但可得性不同。美股公司通常直接披露 EBITDA;A股财报一般不单列这一行,需分析师依据已披露数据自行计算——由于对「加回哪些项目」的处理略有出入,同一家A股公司算出的 EBITDA 可能并不一致。
nodes.formula_display: Net income + Interest + Taxes + Depreciation + Amortization

nodes.definition_en: A company's profit divided by its number of shares, showing how much earnings each share represents. 'Basic' uses shares currently outstanding; 'diluted' also counts shares that could be created from options and convertibles, giving a more conservative figure.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司利润除以股份数量,表示每一股对应多少盈利。「基本」用当前流通股数计算;「稀释」还计入期权、可转债等可能新增的股份,得到更保守的数值。
nodes.formula_display: Net profit attributable to shareholders ÷ Weighted-average shares outstanding

nodes.definition_en: The actual cash a company generates from running its core business over a period — cash collected from customers minus cash paid for operating costs. Unlike profit, it ignores accounting items that don't move cash.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司在一段时期内经营主业实际产生的现金,即向客户收到的现金减去为经营支出付出的现金。与利润不同,它不受不涉及现金流动的会计项目影响。

nodes.definition_en: The cash a company has left after paying for the investments needed to maintain and grow its business — operating cash flow minus capital spending. It's the cash truly available to return to investors or pay down debt.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司在支付维持和扩张业务所需投资之后剩下的现金,即经营性现金流减去资本开支。这是真正可用于回馈投资者或偿还债务的现金。

nodes.definition_en: Money a company spends to buy or upgrade long-lived physical assets like factories, machines, and equipment — investment meant to pay off over many years rather than day-to-day running costs.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司为购置或升级厂房、机器、设备等长期实物资产而花的钱——这是着眼于未来多年回报的投资,而非日常经营开支。

nodes.definition_en: Money customers owe a company for goods or services already delivered but not yet paid for. A fast-rising balance can be a warning that sales are being booked faster than cash is actually collected.
nodes.definition_zh: 客户因已收到商品或服务但尚未付款而欠公司的钱。若这项余额快速上升,可能预示收入确认的速度快过实际收款,是一种风险信号。

nodes.definition_en: The goods a company is holding to sell — raw materials, work in progress, and finished products not yet sold. Rising inventory can signal either preparation for growth or weakening demand.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司为销售而持有的商品,包括原材料、在产品和尚未售出的产成品。存货上升既可能意味着为增长备货,也可能预示需求走弱。

nodes.definition_en: Money a company has already collected from customers for goods or services it hasn't delivered yet — an obligation to deliver, so it counts as a liability until the product is provided. A healthy balance often signals strong advance demand.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司已向客户收取、但相应商品或服务尚未交付的款项——因为还欠着交付义务,在交货前记为负债。余额充裕往往说明预订需求旺盛。

nodes.definition_en: Goodwill is the premium a company pays to acquire another business above the value of its identifiable assets. If the acquired business later underperforms, the company must write down that goodwill — an impairment — which hits reported profit.
nodes.definition_zh: 商誉是公司收购另一家企业时,超出其可辨认资产价值所支付的溢价。若被收购业务后续表现不佳,公司须对商誉计提减值,冲减当期利润。

nodes.definition_en: The cost a company records when it pays employees with shares or options instead of cash. It's a real expense that reduces profit, even though no cash leaves the company.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司用股票或期权而非现金支付员工报酬时所记录的费用。它是一项真实的费用,会减少利润,尽管公司并未实际付出现金。

nodes.definition_en: The gradual spreading of a long-lived asset's cost across the years it's used, rather than expensing it all at once. Depreciation applies to physical assets (machines, buildings); amortization to intangible ones (patents, software).
nodes.definition_zh: 把长期资产的成本分摊到其使用的若干年份,而不是一次性计入费用。折旧针对实物资产(机器、厂房),摊销针对无形资产(专利、软件)。

nodes.zh: 总资产 / 总负债 / 净资产(股东权益)
nodes.definition_en: The three pieces of the balance sheet: assets are everything a company owns, liabilities are everything it owes, and equity is what's left for the owners. They always balance — assets equal liabilities plus equity.
nodes.definition_zh: 资产负债表的三大部分:总资产是公司拥有的一切,总负债是公司欠下的一切,净资产(股东权益)是归属所有者的剩余部分。三者恒等——资产 = 负债 + 权益。

nodes.zh: 美国通用会计准则 / 非GAAP口径
nodes.definition_en: GAAP is the official US accounting rulebook that companies must follow. 'Non-GAAP' figures are alternative, company-adjusted numbers (like adjusted earnings) that management presents alongside GAAP to highlight what it sees as underlying performance.
nodes.definition_zh: GAAP 是美国公司必须遵循的官方会计准则。「非GAAP」是公司自行调整后的另一套数字(如调整后利润),管理层在 GAAP 数据之外一并披露,用来突出其眼中的核心经营表现。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The idea of an 'adjusted' figure sitting beside the official one works differently across the two systems. In the US, non-GAAP is defined by each company and tends to add items back, so it flatters earnings. China has no company-defined non-GAAP culture; its nearest analogue, 扣非净利润, is defined by the regulator and strips items out to be stricter. So a US 'adjusted' number and a Chinese one lean in opposite directions.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 「在官方数字旁再给一套调整后数字」这件事,两套体系做法不同。美国的 non-GAAP 由各公司自行定义,倾向于把项目加回,使利润更好看。中国没有公司自定 non-GAAP 的传统,最接近的扣非净利润由监管定义、剔除项目,反而更严格。因此美式「调整后」与中式「调整后」方向相反。

nodes.definition_en: The official set of accounting rules that companies listed in mainland China (A-shares) must follow when preparing their financial statements.
nodes.definition_zh: 中国大陆上市公司(A股)编制财务报表时必须遵循的官方会计准则体系。
nodes.nuance_note_en: CAS has largely converged with the international IFRS standards, so most items line up — but not all. A few line definitions still differ in ways that matter, most notably operating income (营业利润), which under CAS includes investment gains that US GAAP excludes. So 'converged' does not mean 'identical', and cross-border comparisons still need care.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: CAS 已在很大程度上与国际准则 IFRS 趋同,多数科目可以对应——但并非全部。少数科目的定义仍有实质差异,最典型的是营业利润:CAS 将投资收益计入,而美国 GAAP 不计入。因此「趋同」不等于「等同」,跨境对比仍需谨慎。

nodes.definition_en: A globally used set of accounting standards adopted by many countries and by Hong Kong–listed companies, designed to make financial statements comparable across borders.
nodes.definition_zh: 一套被许多国家和港股上市公司采用的国际通用会计准则,目的是让不同国家的财务报表可以相互比较。

nodes.definition_en: The 12-month period a company uses for its financial reporting, which may or may not match the calendar year.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司用于财务报告的十二个月周期,不一定与自然年(日历年)一致。
nodes.nuance_note_en: In A-shares the fiscal year is always the calendar year, so 'FY2024' unambiguously means January–December 2024. US companies (and US-listed Chinese firms) may close their books on other dates, so a US 'FY2024' can cover a different span — a mismatch to watch when lining up Chinese and US results for the 'same' year.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: A股的财年一律等于自然年,「2024财年」明确就是2024年1—12月。而美股公司(以及在美上市的中概股)财年截止日可能是其他日期,因此美式「FY2024」覆盖的区间可能不同——在对齐中美「同一年」业绩时需特别留意这种错位。

nodes.definition_en: A figure covering the most recent four quarters added together, giving an up-to-date full-year view without waiting for the official year-end. Note it means the trailing four quarters summed — not the latest quarter multiplied by four.
nodes.definition_zh: 把最近四个季度加总得到的数据,无需等到正式年末即可反映最新的「全年」水平。注意它是滚动近四个季度的合计,而非最新一季乘以四。

nodes.definition_en: Two ways to measure change: year-over-year (YoY, 同比) compares a period with the same period a year earlier; quarter-over-quarter (QoQ, 环比) compares it with the immediately preceding quarter.
nodes.definition_zh: 两种衡量变化的方式:同比(YoY)是与去年同期比较;环比(QoQ)是与紧邻的上一个季度比较。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The concepts are standard, but A-share quarterly reports are usually cumulative (year-to-date) rather than single-quarter. To compute a clean quarter-over-quarter change you first have to back out each standalone quarter from the cumulative figures — a step US discrete-quarter reporting doesn't require.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 概念本身是通用的,但A股季报通常是累计数(年初至今),而非单季数。要算出干净的环比,得先从累计数中还原出各个单季——这一步在美股按单季披露的报表里并不需要。

nodes.definition_en: Presenting financial figures as a running total from the start of the year up to the reporting date, rather than as the results of that single quarter alone.
nodes.definition_zh: 把财务数据按年初至报告日的累计总额来列示,而不是只列示当季单独的结果。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Year-to-date figures exist everywhere, but which form is the native one differs. A-share quarterly statements are presented cumulatively by default, so the Q3 report shows nine months combined. US companies report each quarter as a discrete standalone period. Because of this, pulling a single quarter out of Chinese filings takes an extra subtraction step.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 年初至今的数据到处都有,但「哪种形式是原生披露」不同。A股季报默认以累计口径列示,三季报呈现的是前九个月合计。美股则把每个季度作为独立单季披露。正因如此,要从A股报表中取出某个单季数据,需多做一步相减。

nodes.definition_en: Financial statements that combine a parent company and all the subsidiaries it controls into a single set of numbers, as if they were one economic entity.
nodes.definition_zh: 把母公司及其所控制的全部子公司合并为一套数字来编制的财务报表,视同一个经济整体。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Consolidation itself works the same way in both systems, but disclosure practice differs. A-share companies publish two sets — consolidated statements and parent-company-only statements — side by side, and analysts read both. US filers typically present only the consolidated set, so the parent-only view that Chinese readers routinely check has no standard US counterpart.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 合并的方法在两套体系里相同,但披露习惯不同。A股公司会同时披露两套——合并报表和母公司报表,分析时两套都看。美股通常只披露合并报表,因此中国读者常参考的「母公司口径」在美国并无标准对应。

nodes.definition_en: The total market value of a company's shares — the share price multiplied by the number of shares. A quick measure of how big the company is in the eyes of the market.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司全部股份的市场总价值,即股价乘以股份数量。它能快速衡量市场眼中这家公司的大小。
nodes.nuance_note_en: A-shares split market cap into two figures because many shares are restricted from trading: 总市值 (total, counting all shares) and 流通市值 (free-float, counting only freely tradable shares). A US 'market cap' is usually just the single total. So a Chinese company can be quoted with two quite different 'sizes' depending on which one is meant.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 由于A股有大量限售股不能交易,市值被拆成两个:总市值(计入全部股份)和流通市值(只计入可自由交易的股份)。美式「market cap」通常只有总额一个数。因此同一家A股公司,视口径不同可能被报出两个相差很大的「体量」。

nodes.definition_en: What it would cost to buy a whole company outright — its market value plus the debt you'd take on, minus its cash. A truer measure of total worth than market cap alone, since it accounts for debt.
nodes.definition_zh: 买下整家公司实际需要付出的代价——市值加上需承接的债务,再减去公司持有的现金。因为纳入了债务,它比单看市值更能反映公司的整体价值。

nodes.definition_en: How much investors pay for each unit of a company's yearly earnings — the share price divided by earnings per share. A quick gauge of how expensive a stock is relative to the profit it makes.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资者为公司每一单位年度盈利所支付的价格,即股价除以每股收益,用来快速衡量股票相对其盈利的贵贱。
nodes.nuance_note_en: In Chinese usage 市盈率 splits into three versions that give different numbers for the same stock on the same day: 静态 (using the last full fiscal year's earnings), 动态 (using this year's forecast earnings), and 滚动/TTM (using the trailing four quarters). English 'trailing P/E' and 'forward P/E' line up only loosely with 静态 and 动态, so a quoted 市盈率 and a quoted P/E can differ purely because of which earnings window each one uses.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 中文「市盈率」常分为三种口径,同一只股票在同一天算出的数值并不相同:静态用上一完整财年的盈利,动态用当年预测盈利,滚动(TTM)用最近四个季度。英文的 trailing P/E、forward P/E 与静态、动态并非严格对应,因此报出的市盈率与 P/E 可能仅仅因为所取的盈利区间不同而产生差异。

nodes.zh: 预测市盈率 / 滚动市盈率
nodes.definition_en: Two versions of the P/E ratio that differ by which earnings they use: forward P/E uses analysts' forecast of future earnings, while trailing P/E uses earnings already reported over the past year.
nodes.definition_zh: 市盈率的两种版本,区别在于用哪一段盈利:预测(forward)市盈率用分析师对未来盈利的预测,滚动(trailing)市盈率用过去一年已披露的盈利。
nodes.nuance_note_en: These English terms don't map cleanly onto the Chinese 动态/静态 pair. Forward P/E is forecast-based like 动态, and trailing P/E is backward-looking like 滚动, but 静态 (last full fiscal year only) has no exact English label. So translating 'forward' as 动态 or 'trailing' as 静态 can quietly shift which earnings window is meant.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这组英文术语与中文的动态/静态并非严格对应。预测市盈率像动态一样基于预测,滚动市盈率像 trailing 一样回看过去,但「静态」(仅用上一完整财年)在英文里没有确切对应词。因此把 forward 译作动态、trailing 译作静态,可能悄悄改变所指的盈利区间。

nodes.definition_en: A company's market value divided by its revenue. It's used to value companies that aren't yet profitable — often fast-growing firms — where a P/E ratio wouldn't work because there are no earnings to divide by.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司市值除以营业收入。它常用于尚未盈利的公司(往往是高速成长股),因为这类公司没有盈利可供相除,市盈率无法使用。

nodes.definition_en: A company's market value divided by its book value (net assets). Common for banks and cyclical firms. When the ratio falls below 1, the stock trades below book value — in Chinese, 破净.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司市值除以账面价值(净资产)。常用于银行和周期性公司。当该比率低于1时,股价跌破净资产,中文称为「破净」。
nodes.formula_display: Market cap ÷ Book value of equity

nodes.definition_en: A valuation ratio comparing a company's total value (enterprise value) with its pre-financing, pre-tax cash earnings (EBITDA). Favored for capital-heavy industries because it looks past differences in debt and tax.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种估值比率,用公司整体价值(企业价值)对比其融资、税前的现金利润(EBITDA)。因能跳过债务与税负差异,常用于资本密集型行业。中文常直接沿用英文 EV/EBITDA。

nodes.definition_en: The P/E ratio divided by the company's earnings growth rate. It puts a stock's price tag in the context of how fast it's growing — a high P/E can look reasonable if growth is fast enough.
nodes.definition_zh: 市盈率除以公司的盈利增速。它把股票的估值放到成长速度的背景下看——只要增长足够快,较高的市盈率也可能显得合理。

nodes.definition_en: A method of valuing a company by estimating the cash it will generate in future years and translating that back into today's money, since a dollar in the future is worth less than a dollar now.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种给公司估值的方法:先预测公司未来各年产生的现金,再把它折算回今天的价值——因为未来的一元钱不如现在的一元钱值钱。

nodes.definition_en: The average rate of return a company must pay to fund itself, blending the cost of its debt and the cost of its equity. It's commonly used as the discount rate in a DCF valuation.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司为筹集资金而必须支付的平均回报率,将债务成本与股权成本加权综合而成。在现金流折现估值中,它常被用作折现率。

nodes.definition_en: The annual dividend a stock pays expressed as a percentage of its share price — how much cash income you earn each year for every unit invested, before any price change.
nodes.definition_zh: 股票每年派发的股息占其股价的百分比——即在股价涨跌之外,每投入一单位资金每年能拿到多少现金收益。

nodes.definition_en: The share of a company's profit that it pays out to shareholders as dividends, rather than keeping to reinvest. A 40% payout ratio means 40 cents of every dollar earned is distributed.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司把利润中多少比例作为股息派发给股东,而非留存再投资。分红率40%意味着每赚一元派发四角。

nodes.definition_en: How much profit a company generates for each unit of shareholders' equity — net income divided by equity. A basic read on how efficiently a company puts its owners' money to work.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司用每一单位股东权益所创造的利润,即净利润除以净资产,用来基本判断公司运用股东资金的效率。
nodes.nuance_note_en: A-share reporting publishes ROE in two forms that come out to different numbers: 加权 (dividing by the weighted-average equity held over the period) and 摊薄 (dividing by period-end equity). Chinese regulators highlight the weighted version in official filings, whereas a casually quoted US ROE is usually the simpler period-end calculation — so a 净资产收益率 and an ROE for the same firm can differ just because of which equity base is used.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: A股财报会披露两种数值不同的净资产收益率:加权(以期间加权平均净资产为分母)和摊薄(以期末净资产为分母)。中国监管在正式披露中突出加权口径,而美式随口引用的 ROE 多为较简单的期末口径——因此同一家公司的净资产收益率与 ROE 可能仅因所用净资产基准不同而有别。

nodes.zh: 总资产收益率 / 投入资本回报率
nodes.definition_en: Two efficiency measures. ROA (return on assets) shows profit generated per unit of total assets; ROIC (return on invested capital) shows profit per unit of the capital — debt plus equity — actually put to work in the business.
nodes.definition_zh: 两个衡量效率的指标。总资产收益率(ROA)反映每单位总资产创造的利润;投入资本回报率(ROIC)反映每单位实际投入经营的资本(债务加股权)所创造的利润。

nodes.definition_en: A way of redrawing a stock's historical price chart so past prices are restated to match today's price basis, keeping the line continuous through dividends and share changes. Useful for reading candlestick charts without artificial gaps.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种重绘股票历史价格的方式:以现价为基准调整历史价格,使股价曲线在分红、送转等事件处保持连续。看K线连续性时使用。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The underlying idea — adjusting past prices for dividends and splits — exists in every market. What's distinctive is that Chinese trading platforms make it an explicit three-way choice: 前复权 (adjust to current basis), 后复权 (adjust to the listing basis), and 不复权 (raw). US charting tools usually just show a single 'adjusted' series, so the deliberate 前/后 distinction has no direct counterpart.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 「为分红、拆股调整历史价格」这一思路各市场都有。特别之处在于,中国交易软件把它做成明确的三选一:前复权(调至现价基准)、后复权(调至上市基准)、不复权(原始价)。美国看盘工具通常只给单一「调整后」序列,因此这种前/后之分并无直接对应。

nodes.definition_en: The mirror image of forward adjustment: historical prices are held at their original listing basis and later prices are scaled up instead. Useful for measuring a stock's true long-run return from listing to now.
nodes.definition_zh: 与前复权相反:以上市价为基准保持历史价格不变,转而向上调整之后的价格。用于计算股票从上市至今的真实长期收益率。
nodes.nuance_note_en: As with forward adjustment, the concept of adjusting for corporate actions is universal, but the explicit 后复权 mode is part of a Chinese-platform three-way toggle (前/后/不复权). A US 'adjusted close' series behaves most like a back-adjusted line, but it isn't labeled or offered as a separate selectable mode the way it is on Chinese platforms.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 与前复权一样,「按公司行为调整」的概念各处通用,但明确的后复权模式属于中国软件的三选一(前/后/不复权)。美国的「adjusted close」序列在效果上最接近后复权,但它并不像中国软件那样被单列为一个可选模式。

nodes.definition_en: The actual price a stock traded at on each historical date, with no adjustment for dividends or share changes. Because those events reset the price, an unadjusted chart isn't directly comparable across them.
nodes.definition_zh: 股票在历史上每一天实际成交的价格,不对分红或送转做任何调整。由于这些事件会重置价格,不复权的图形在事件前后不可直接比较。

nodes.definition_en: The moment a stock's price is mechanically reduced to reflect a payout or share change: ex-dividend drops the price by the cash dividend paid, ex-rights adjusts it for bonus or rights shares. This price reset is exactly why charts need adjustment.
nodes.definition_zh: 股价因派发或股本变动而被机械下调的时点:除息按派发的现金股息下调股价,除权按送股或配股调整股价。正是这种价格重置,才需要对图形进行复权。

nodes.definition_en: Handing shareholders extra shares instead of cash: 送股 pays them out of retained earnings, 转增 issues them from capital reserves. Either way you own more shares, each worth proportionally less, so your total value is unchanged.
nodes.definition_zh: 向股东派发额外股份而非现金:送股来自留存利润,转增来自资本公积。无论哪种,你持有的股份变多、每股相应变便宜,总价值不变。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Bonus/scrip issues exist in many markets, but China's version has two distinct sources (retained earnings vs capital reserves) and drives a distinctive speculative theme, 高送转 (large bonus-and-transfer packages). Mechanically these events rebase every past per-share figure — EPS, price history — so historical data must be re-stated, a heavier footprint than an ordinary US stock dividend usually leaves.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 送股这类操作许多市场都有,但中国版有两种不同来源(留存利润 vs 资本公积),并催生出独特的炒作题材——高送转。机制上,这类事件会重置全部历史 per-share 数据(每股收益、价格历史),因此历史数据须整体还原,其影响比美式普通股票股利通常留下的痕迹更大。

nodes.definition_en: When a company uses its own cash to buy back its shares from the market, reducing the number of shares outstanding and returning value to the shareholders who remain.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司动用自有资金从市场上买回自家股票,减少流通在外的股份数量,并借此向留存下来的股东回馈价值。

nodes.definition_en: Ways a listed company raises fresh money by issuing new shares: a secondary offering sells them broadly to the market, while a private placement (定增) sells them to a small set of chosen investors.
nodes.definition_zh: 上市公司通过发行新股再融资的方式:增发面向市场公开发售新股,定向增发(定增)则把新股卖给少数特定投资者。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The tools exist in both markets, but their weighting differs. In A-shares, 定增 (targeted private placement) is the dominant refinancing channel, governed by specific rules on pricing, eligible investors, and lock-up periods. US listed companies more often raise follow-on capital through broad public secondary offerings; the tightly-regulated 定增 model is the A-share default rather than the exception.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这些工具两地都有,但主次不同。在A股,定向增发(定增)是再融资的主流渠道,对定价、认购对象、锁定期都有专门规定。美股上市公司更常通过面向公众的公开增发募资;而受严格监管的定增模式在A股是常态而非例外。

nodes.definition_en: Dividing each existing share into several, lowering the price per share while leaving the company's total value unchanged — often done to keep a high-priced stock affordable to small investors.
nodes.definition_zh: 把每一股拆分成若干股,降低每股价格,而公司总价值不变——通常是为了让高价股对散户保持可负担。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Conventional stock splits are common in the US but rare in A-shares. Chinese companies achieve a similar effect — more shares, lower price each — through bonus issues and transfers (送转) instead. So the American 'stock split' and the Chinese 送转 aim at the same outcome by different mechanisms, and 送转 additionally shifts value from reserves rather than being a pure re-slicing.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 传统意义的拆股在美股常见,在A股则少见。中国公司通过送股、转增(送转)来达到类似效果——股份变多、每股变便宜。因此美式「stock split」与中式「送转」目标相同但机制不同,而送转还额外涉及从公积金转出价值,并非纯粹的股份再切分。

nodes.definition_en: When a company separates part of its business into a new, independently listed company, so the unit trades on its own rather than as one line inside the parent.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司把旗下某块业务分离出来、独立上市,使该业务作为一家独立公司单独交易,而不再只是母公司里的一部分。

nodes.definition_en: Deals in which companies combine or one buys another — merging two firms into one, or acquiring control of a target. Often also bundled with restructuring of the combined business.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司之间合并或一方收购另一方的交易——把两家公司合为一家,或取得目标公司的控制权,通常还伴随对合并后业务的重组。

nodes.definition_en: The date on which previously restricted shares — held by insiders or early investors and barred from sale for a set period — become free to trade, potentially adding selling pressure.
nodes.definition_zh: 此前受限的股份(由内部人或早期投资者持有、在约定期限内不得出售)变得可以自由交易的日期,可能带来抛售压力。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Lock-ups exist in the US too, typically the 90–180 days after an IPO. But in A-shares 限售股解禁 is a far broader and more closely-watched risk event: large blocks of restricted shares from many sources come free on scheduled dates, and the resulting 'overhang' is a routine part of A-share risk analysis in a way post-IPO lock-ups usually aren't in the US.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美股也有锁定期,通常是IPO后的90—180天。但在A股,限售股解禁是范围更广、也被更密切关注的风险事件:来自多种来源的大量限售股在既定日期集中解禁,由此形成的「解禁压力」是A股风险分析的常规环节,而美股的IPO锁定期通常没有这样的分量。

nodes.definition_en: When a company's large shareholders — founders, controlling owners, or major backers — sell down their stakes, often read by the market as a negative signal about the stock.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司的大股东(创始人、控股方或重要股东)减少所持股份的行为,市场往往将其解读为对该股票的负面信号。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Insiders sell in every market, and the US discloses it through Form 4 filings. But 大股东减持 is a distinct, high-frequency risk theme in A-shares, with its own disclosure rules, pre-announcement requirements, and pace limits, and it draws intense retail attention. The same underlying act carries more market weight and a heavier regulatory apparatus in China than routine US insider sales.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 内部人减持各市场都有,美国通过 Form 4 披露。但大股东减持在A股是一个独特且高频的风险主题,有专门的披露规则、预披露要求和节奏限制,并高度牵动散户情绪。同样的行为在中国比美国常规的内部人卖出承载更重的市场分量和更严的监管框架。

nodes.definition_en: When a major shareholder pledges their shares as collateral for a loan. If the share price falls far enough, the lender can force a sale of the pledged shares, which can accelerate a stock's decline.
nodes.definition_zh: 大股东把所持股份作为抵押品去借款。若股价跌得足够深,贷款方可强制卖出质押股份,可能加速股价下跌。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Pledging shares for a loan happens everywhere, but in A-shares 股权质押 is tracked as a systemic risk indicator. High pledge ratios among controlling shareholders were a widespread stress point, so the market monitors aggregate pledging levels closely — a lens that has no equally prominent equivalent in US markets.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 以股份质押借款各处都有,但在A股,股权质押被当作系统性风险指标来跟踪。控股股东高比例质押曾是普遍的风险点,因此市场密切关注整体质押水平——这种视角在美国市场并无同等突出的对应。

nodes.definition_en: A company's own forecast of its upcoming financial results — such as expected revenue or profit for the next quarter or year — offered to help investors set expectations.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司对自身未来财务结果(如下一季度或全年的预期收入、利润)给出的预测,用来帮助投资者形成预期。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Voluntary forward guidance is routine in the US. In A-shares it's uncommon; the partly-overlapping role is played by the mandatory 业绩预告 (earnings pre-announcement) system, which is triggered by rules rather than offered at management's discretion. So a US reader expecting company-issued 'guidance' should look instead to China's rule-based pre-disclosure regime.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 自愿性的前瞻业绩指引在美国是常规做法。在A股则不常见,与之部分重叠的角色由强制性的业绩预告制度承担,它由规则触发,而非管理层自主给出。因此期待公司主动发布「guidance」的美国读者,在中国应转而关注这套按规则触发的预披露制度。

nodes.definition_en: An early heads-up about a company's upcoming results, released before the full report — flagging, for example, that profit will rise or fall sharply versus a year earlier.
nodes.definition_zh: 在正式财报公布前,公司对即将发布的业绩给出的提前提示——例如预告利润将同比大幅上升或下降。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Early earnings signals happen in the US too, but usually as voluntary profit warnings. China's 业绩预告 is different in kind: it's a mandatory pre-disclosure regime, where companies crossing certain profit-change thresholds are required to announce ahead of the full report. So the same idea — an early earnings signal — is discretionary in the US but rule-driven and compulsory in China.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 提前释放业绩信号在美国也有,但通常是自愿性的盈利预警。中国的业绩预告性质不同:它是强制性的预披露制度,凡利润变动跨过特定阈值的公司,须在正式财报前发布预告。因此同样是「提前的业绩信号」,在美国是自主选择,在中国却是按规则强制执行。

nodes.definition_en: A meeting — usually by phone or webcast — where a company's management presents its latest results and answers questions from analysts and investors.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司管理层介绍最新业绩并回答分析师和投资者提问的会议,通常以电话或网络直播形式举行。

nodes.definition_en: Whether a company's reported results come in above ('beat') or below ('miss') what analysts had expected. Because prices already reflect expectations, the beat or miss often moves the stock more than the raw numbers.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司实际业绩相对分析师预期是高于(超预期)还是低于(不及预期)。由于股价已反映预期,超预期或不及预期往往比数字本身更能推动股价。

nodes.definition_en: The average of analysts' forecasts for a company's results — the market's collective expectation, against which actual results are judged as a beat or miss. Chinese data platforms such as Wind compile their own consensus figures.
nodes.definition_zh: 分析师对公司业绩预测的平均值——即市场的集体预期,实际业绩相对它判定为超预期或不及预期。中文数据源(如 Wind)会自行汇编一致预期。

nodes.definition_en: Owning an asset in the expectation that its price will rise, so you profit when it goes up. Being 'long' a stock simply means you hold it and want it to climb.
nodes.definition_zh: 持有某项资产,预期其价格上涨,从而在上涨时获利。「做多」一只股票,就是持有它并希望它上涨。

nodes.definition_en: Betting that a price will fall — typically by borrowing an asset, selling it now, and buying it back cheaper later to return it. You profit if the price drops.
nodes.definition_zh: 押注价格下跌——通常是借入资产、现在卖出,之后以更低价格买回归还。若价格下跌即可获利。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The concept is identical, but the feasibility differs sharply. In the US, borrowing shares to short is easy and routine. In A-shares, shorting depends on securities lending (融券), which is tightly restricted, limited to eligible stocks, and often expensive or unavailable — so shorting is far harder to execute in practice.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 概念完全相同,但可行性差别很大。在美股,借券做空既容易又常见。在A股,做空依赖融券,而融券受到严格限制,仅限特定标的,且常常成本高或根本借不到——因此做空在实操中难得多。

nodes.definition_en: How much of an asset you currently hold. Chinese trading slang describes position size vividly: 满仓 (fully invested), 空仓 (all in cash), 加仓 (adding), 减仓 (trimming).
nodes.definition_zh: 你当前持有某项资产的规模。中文交易黑话对仓位描述生动:满仓、空仓、加仓、减仓,分别对应 fully invested / all cash / add / trim。

nodes.definition_en: An instruction to buy or sell immediately at whatever the current market price is. It prioritizes getting the trade done over getting a specific price.
nodes.definition_zh: 以当前市场价格立即买入或卖出的指令。它优先保证成交,而不追求某个具体价格。

nodes.definition_en: An instruction to buy or sell only at a specified price or better. It guarantees your price but not that the trade will execute — if the market never reaches your price, nothing happens.
nodes.definition_zh: 只在指定价格或更优价格才成交的买卖指令。它保证价格但不保证成交——若市场从未触及你的价格,就不会成交。

nodes.definition_en: A preset order to sell automatically once a price falls to a chosen level, capping your loss on a trade. Its mirror image is 止盈 (take profit), which locks in gains at a target price.
nodes.definition_zh: 预先设定的指令,一旦价格跌到选定水平便自动卖出,以限制该笔交易的亏损。与之相对的是止盈(take profit),即在目标价位锁定收益。

nodes.definition_en: The two sides of a quote: the bid is the highest price buyers are willing to pay, the ask is the lowest price sellers will accept. The best bid and best ask are called 买一 / 卖一 in Chinese.
nodes.definition_zh: 报价的两侧:买价是买方愿意支付的最高价,卖价是卖方愿意接受的最低价。最优买价、最优卖价在中文里称为「买一 / 卖一」。

nodes.definition_en: The gap between the bid and ask prices. A narrow spread signals a liquid, easily traded market; a wide one means trading costs more because you buy a bit high and sell a bit low.
nodes.definition_zh: 买价与卖价之间的差距。价差窄说明市场流动性好、易于交易;价差宽则意味着交易成本更高,因为你买得略贵、卖得略便宜。

nodes.definition_en: The difference between the price you expected to trade at and the price you actually got. It happens when the market moves or lacks depth between placing and filling an order, and it eats into returns.
nodes.definition_zh: 你预期成交的价格与实际成交价格之间的偏差。当下单到成交之间市场发生波动或深度不足时就会出现,会侵蚀收益。

nodes.definition_en: How easily an asset can be bought or sold without moving its price much. A highly liquid stock has plenty of buyers and sellers, so you can trade large amounts quickly at a fair price.
nodes.definition_zh: 一项资产在不明显影响其价格的情况下被买卖的难易程度。流动性高的股票买卖双方充足,因此可以快速、以合理价格成交大额交易。

nodes.definition_en: How much trading is happening: volume (成交量) counts shares traded, turnover (成交额) counts their money value, and turnover rate (换手率) measures traded shares as a percentage of those available — a gauge of how actively a stock changes hands.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量交易活跃度:成交量(shares)数股数,成交额(value)数金额,换手率(turnover rate)则是成交股数占可交易股数的百分比,反映一只股票换手的频繁程度。

nodes.definition_en: Rules about how soon after buying you can sell. Under T+1, shares bought today cannot be sold until the next trading day; under T+0, you can buy and sell the same day.
nodes.definition_zh: 关于买入后多快可以卖出的规则。T+1 下,今天买入的股票要到下一交易日才能卖出;T+0 下,可以当天买入当天卖出。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Beware the same label meaning different things. In the A-share context, 'T+1' refers to this no-same-day-sell trading rule for stocks (bonds and convertibles trade T+0). In US usage, 'T+1' usually refers to the settlement cycle — when cash and shares actually change hands — while intraday round-trip trading is freely allowed. So the same 'T+1' points at a trading restriction in China and a settlement timeline in the US.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 小心同一标签指代不同事物。在A股语境下,「T+1」指股票不能当日买卖的交易规则(债券、可转债则是 T+0)。在美国用法里,「T+1」通常指结算周期——现金和股份实际交割的时间——而当日来回交易是自由允许的。因此同样是「T+1」,在中国指交易限制,在美国指结算时点。

nodes.definition_en: A cap on how far a stock's price may move up or down in a single trading day. Once it hits the limit, trading can continue only within that band — a hard ceiling (涨停) or floor (跌停) for the day.
nodes.definition_zh: 对一只股票单个交易日价格涨跌幅度的限制。一旦触及限幅,当天只能在该范围内交易——形成当日的硬性上限(涨停)或下限(跌停)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The idea of limiting daily moves exists in the US too, but as market-wide, temporary circuit-breaker halts. A-shares apply a fixed per-stock daily band every day — ±10% on the main board, ±20% on ChiNext and the STAR Market — so an individual stock can be locked 'limit up' or 'limit down' with no trades clearing. That standing per-stock cap has no direct US equivalent.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 限制单日涨跌的思路美国也有,但表现为全市场、临时性的熔断停牌。A股则对每只股票每天施加固定的涨跌幅——主板±10%,创业板、科创板±20%——因此个股可能被封在涨停或跌停、无法成交。这种常态化的个股涨跌停在美国并无直接对应。

nodes.definition_en: A trading method where orders collected over a short window are matched all at once at a single price, rather than continuously. It's typically used to set the opening and closing prices of the day.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种交易方式:把一小段时间内收集的委托一次性以单一价格集中撮合,而非连续成交。通常用于确定当天的开盘价和收盘价。

nodes.definition_en: Trading with borrowed money or borrowed securities: borrowing cash to buy more than you could afford (融资), or borrowing shares to sell short (融券). It amplifies both gains and losses.
nodes.definition_zh: 用借来的资金或证券进行交易:借入资金买入超出自有能力的仓位(融资),或借入股票卖空(融券)。它会放大盈利,也放大亏损。

nodes.definition_en: The behind-the-scenes process that finalizes a trade — the buyer's cash and the seller's shares actually change hands and ownership is officially recorded, usually a day or two after the trade is agreed.
nodes.definition_zh: 交易达成后在幕后完成的最终环节——买方的资金与卖方的股份实际交割,所有权正式登记,通常在成交后一两天完成。

nodes.definition_en: The return an investment earns above and beyond its benchmark — the part of performance attributed to skill rather than simply riding the market. Positive alpha means you beat the benchmark.
nodes.definition_zh: 一项投资相对其基准所多赚的收益——即归因于能力而非单纯跟随市场的那部分表现。正的阿尔法意味着跑赢了基准。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of how much an asset tends to move with the overall market. A beta of 1 means it moves in step; above 1, it swings more than the market; below 1, less.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量一项资产随大盘波动的程度。贝塔为1表示与市场同步;高于1表示波动大于市场;低于1表示波动小于市场。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of return earned per unit of risk taken — an investment's return above the risk-free rate, divided by how much it bounces around. Higher is better: more reward for each unit of volatility.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量每承担一单位风险所获得的收益——即投资高于无风险利率的收益,除以其波动程度。数值越高越好,表示每单位波动换来的回报越多。

nodes.definition_en: A cousin of the Sharpe ratio that only penalizes downside volatility, ignoring upside swings. The idea is that investors don't mind big moves up — only big moves down.
nodes.definition_zh: 夏普比率的近亲,只对下行波动进行「惩罚」,忽略上行波动。其逻辑是:投资者并不介意大幅上涨,只担心大幅下跌。

nodes.definition_en: The largest peak-to-trough drop an investment suffered over a period — how much you would have lost if you bought at the worst high and sold at the following low. A gut-check on worst-case pain.
nodes.definition_zh: 一项投资在一段时期内从最高点到随后最低点的最大跌幅——即在最糟的高点买入、在其后低点卖出会亏多少。它直观反映最坏情况下的痛苦程度。

nodes.definition_en: How much and how fast a price swings around. High volatility means large, rapid moves in either direction. 'Implied volatility' (IV) is the market's forecast of future volatility, read from option prices.
nodes.definition_zh: 价格上下波动的幅度和速度。波动率高意味着价格在两个方向上都大幅、快速地变动。「隐含波动率」(IV)是从期权价格反推出的市场对未来波动的预期。

nodes.definition_en: A return restated as an equivalent yearly rate, so investments held for different lengths of time can be compared on the same footing. Distinct from cumulative return, which is the total gain over the whole period.
nodes.definition_zh: 把收益换算成等效的年度回报率,使持有期长短不同的投资可以在同一标准下比较。它区别于累计收益(cumulative return),后者是整个期间的总收益。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of how consistently a manager beats a benchmark — the excess return over the benchmark divided by how variable that excess is. Used to judge the quality of active management.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量管理人跑赢基准是否稳定的指标——即相对基准的超额收益,除以该超额收益的波动程度。用于评价主动管理的质量。

nodes.definition_en: The share of trades that end in a profit. It's only half the picture — a strategy can have a low win rate yet still make money if its wins are much larger than its losses (a high profit/loss ratio, 盈亏比).
nodes.definition_zh: 盈利交易占全部交易的比例。它只反映一半情况——即便胜率不高,只要盈利远大于亏损(盈亏比高),策略仍可能赚钱。

nodes.definition_en: A standard yardstick — usually a market index like the CSI 300 or S&P 500 — against which an investment's performance is measured to see whether it added value.
nodes.definition_zh: 一把标准的衡量尺子——通常是市场指数,如沪深300或标普500——用来对照投资表现,判断它是否创造了价值。

nodes.definition_en: Taking an offsetting position to reduce the risk of an existing one — like buying insurance. If one holding loses money, the hedge is designed to gain, cushioning the overall impact.
nodes.definition_zh: 建立一个反向仓位来降低已有仓位的风险——如同买保险。当一项持仓亏损时,对冲仓位设计上会获利,从而缓冲整体影响。

nodes.definition_en: Using borrowed money to increase the size of an investment. It magnifies returns when things go well and losses when they don't, so a small price move can have an outsized effect on your capital.
nodes.definition_zh: 用借来的资金放大投资规模。顺利时放大收益,不顺时放大亏损,因此价格的小幅变动就可能对本金产生放大的影响。

nodes.definition_en: How much you stand to gain or lose from a particular risk — a stock, sector, or currency. Net exposure (净敞口) nets your long positions against your short ones to show your true directional bet.
nodes.definition_zh: 你在某一特定风险(某只股票、某个行业或某种货币)上可能盈亏的程度。净敞口(net exposure)是多头与空头相抵后的结果,反映你真正的方向性押注。

nodes.definition_en: A financial contract whose value is derived from something else — a stock, index, commodity, or rate. Options and futures are common examples; you trade the contract rather than the underlying thing itself.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种金融合约,其价值来自其他标的——某只股票、指数、商品或利率。期权和期货是常见例子;你交易的是合约本身,而非其背后的标的。

nodes.definition_en: A contract giving the right, not the obligation, to buy or sell an asset at a set price before a set date.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种合约,赋予持有人在约定日期前以约定价格买卖某资产的权利,但无义务。

nodes.definition_en: An option that gives the right to buy an asset at a set price before a set date. You buy a call when you expect the price to rise.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种赋予以约定价格、在约定日期前买入资产权利的期权。当你预期价格上涨时买入看涨期权。

nodes.definition_en: An option that gives the right to sell an asset at a set price before a set date. You buy a put when you expect the price to fall, or to protect a holding.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种赋予以约定价格、在约定日期前卖出资产权利的期权。当你预期价格下跌、或为保护持仓时买入看跌期权。

nodes.definition_en: The fixed price at which an option lets you buy or sell the underlying asset. Whether an option is worth exercising depends on how the market price compares with this strike.
nodes.definition_zh: 期权约定的、可据以买入或卖出标的资产的固定价格。期权是否值得行权,取决于市场价格与这个行权价的高低关系。

nodes.definition_en: The date on which an option or futures contract ends. After it, the contract no longer exists — an option must be exercised by then or it expires worthless.
nodes.definition_zh: 期权或期货合约结束的日期。到期之后合约不再存在——期权须在此之前行权,否则到期作废、价值归零。

nodes.definition_en: The price a buyer pays to own an option — the cost of acquiring the right it grants. If the option is never exercised, this premium is the most the buyer can lose.
nodes.definition_zh: 买方为持有期权而支付的价格——即取得该项权利的成本。若期权始终未行权,这笔权利金就是买方最多的损失。

nodes.zh: 实值 / 平值 / 虚值
nodes.definition_en: How an option stands relative to the current price: in-the-money (ITM) would profit if exercised now, at-the-money (ATM) sits right at the strike, and out-of-the-money (OTM) has no exercise value yet.
nodes.definition_zh: 期权相对当前价格的状态:实值(ITM)现在行权即可获利,平值(ATM)正处在行权价上,虚值(OTM)目前行权没有价值。

nodes.definition_en: A set of measures showing how an option's price reacts to different forces: Delta to the underlying price, Gamma to changes in Delta, Theta to the passage of time, and Vega to volatility.
nodes.definition_zh: 一组衡量期权价格对各种因素敏感度的指标:Delta 对应标的价格,Gamma 对应 Delta 的变化,Theta 对应时间流逝,Vega 对应波动率。

nodes.definition_en: A standardized contract to buy or sell an asset at a set price on a set future date. Unlike an option, both sides are obligated to complete the trade; widely used to hedge or speculate on commodities, indices, and rates.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种标准化合约,约定在未来某个日期以约定价格买卖资产。与期权不同,买卖双方都有义务完成交易;广泛用于对商品、指数、利率进行对冲或投机。

nodes.definition_en: Futures contracts based on a stock market index rather than a single stock, letting traders bet on or hedge the direction of the whole market. In China the main contracts are IF, IC, IM, and IH, tracking the CSI 300, CSI 500, CSI 1000, and SSE 50.
nodes.definition_zh: 以股票市场指数(而非单只股票)为标的的期货合约,让交易者押注或对冲整个市场的方向。中国主要合约为 IF、IC、IM、IH,分别跟踪沪深300、中证500、中证1000、上证50。

nodes.definition_en: The gap between a futures price and the current price of the underlying. When the future trades below spot it's at a discount (贴水); when above, at a premium (升水). The gap narrows to zero as the contract nears expiry.
nodes.definition_zh: 期货价格与标的现货价格之间的差距。期货低于现货时为贴水(discount),高于现货时为升水(premium)。随着合约临近到期,这一差距收敛至零。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Basis exists in every futures market, but its behavior in Chinese index futures is a distinctive, closely-watched cost. Because shorting stocks is restricted, hedgers crowd into short index futures, pushing them to a persistent and sometimes large discount (贴水) to spot. That standing discount is a core drag on quant hedging strategies in China — a structural feature that isn't as pronounced in most US index futures.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 基差在任何期货市场都有,但在中国股指期货中,它是一个独特且被密切关注的成本。由于股票做空受限,套保者集中做空股指期货,使其相对现货长期、有时大幅贴水。这种持续的贴水是中国量化对冲策略的核心拖累——一种在多数美国股指期货中并不那么显著的结构性特征。

nodes.definition_en: A private, customized agreement to buy or sell an asset at a set price on a future date. Like a futures contract but arranged directly between two parties rather than traded on an exchange.
nodes.definition_zh: 一份私下定制的协议,约定在未来某日以约定价格买卖资产。它类似期货,但由交易双方直接约定,而非在交易所挂牌交易。

nodes.definition_en: A contract in which two parties agree to exchange streams of payments — for example, swapping a floating interest rate for a fixed one. Used to manage exposure to rates, currencies, or other variables.
nodes.definition_zh: 两方约定互相交换一系列现金流的合约——例如把浮动利率换成固定利率。用于管理利率、汇率或其他变量的风险敞口。

nodes.definition_en: A contract that pays the difference between an asset's price when you open and close the position, letting you profit from price moves without owning the asset itself.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种合约,按你开仓与平仓时资产价格之差进行结算,使你无需实际持有资产即可从价格波动中获利。

nodes.zh: 保证金 / 追加保证金
nodes.definition_en: Margin is the collateral you must post to hold a leveraged position. If losses eat into it, the broker issues a margin call demanding more; fail to top up and your position is forcibly closed — in Chinese slang, 爆仓.
nodes.definition_zh: 保证金是持有杠杆仓位须缴纳的抵押资金。若亏损侵蚀保证金,券商会发出追加保证金通知要求补足;补不上,仓位就被强制平仓——中文俗称「爆仓」。

nodes.definition_en: Trading driven by mathematical models and computer programs rather than human judgment. Rules for what to buy and sell are derived from data and executed automatically, often at high speed and scale.
nodes.definition_zh: 由数学模型和计算机程序、而非人的主观判断驱动的交易。买卖规则从数据中提炼,并自动执行,通常速度快、规模大。

nodes.definition_en: A form of quantitative trading that executes huge numbers of orders in fractions of a second, profiting from tiny price differences. Speed of computers and connections is the main edge.
nodes.definition_zh: 量化交易的一种,在极短时间内(几分之一秒)执行海量委托,从微小的价差中获利。计算机与网络的速度是其主要优势。

nodes.definition_en: Building portfolios around measurable characteristics that have historically driven returns — such as momentum, value, quality, low volatility, or small size — rather than picking individual stocks by story.
nodes.definition_zh: 围绕历史上驱动收益的可量化特征(如动量、价值、质量、低波动、小市值)来构建组合,而非凭个股「故事」逐只挑选。

nodes.definition_en: The tendency of assets that have been rising to keep rising, and fallers to keep falling, for a while. A momentum strategy buys recent winners and avoids or shorts recent losers.
nodes.definition_zh: 近期上涨的资产往往在一段时间内继续上涨、下跌的继续下跌的倾向。动量策略买入近期赢家,回避或做空近期输家。

nodes.definition_en: The idea that prices stretched far from their average tend to snap back toward it. A mean-reversion strategy bets against extremes — buying what's fallen unusually and selling what's risen unusually.
nodes.definition_zh: 价格一旦大幅偏离其平均水平,往往会回摆到均值附近的理念。均值回归策略押注极端会修正——买入异常下跌的,卖出异常上涨的。

nodes.definition_en: Profiting from a price difference for the same or related assets in different places or forms, ideally with little risk. Statistical arbitrage (stat arb) applies the idea across many positions using models.
nodes.definition_zh: 利用同一或相关资产在不同场所或不同形式下的价差获利,理想情况下风险很小。统计套利(stat arb)借助模型把这一思路应用到大量仓位上。

nodes.definition_en: Continuously quoting both a buy and a sell price for an asset, profiting from the spread between them while providing liquidity to other traders who want to trade immediately.
nodes.definition_zh: 对一项资产持续同时报出买价和卖价,从买卖价差中获利,同时为需要即时成交的其他交易者提供流动性。

nodes.definition_en: A strategy that rides established price trends — buying when a market is trending up and selling or shorting when it's trending down — on the assumption trends persist rather than trying to predict turning points.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种顺应既有价格趋势的策略——市场上行时买入,下行时卖出或做空——其假设是趋势会延续,而非试图预测拐点。

nodes.definition_en: Trading two related assets that usually move together: when their prices diverge, you buy the laggard and short the leader, betting the gap will close. A market-neutral form of mean reversion.
nodes.definition_zh: 交易两项通常同向变动的相关资产:当它们的价格背离时,买入落后者、做空领先者,押注价差将收敛。这是一种市场中性的均值回归。

nodes.definition_en: Testing a trading strategy on historical data to see how it would have performed. A common trap is look-ahead bias (未来函数) — accidentally using information that wouldn't have been available at the time, which flatters results.
nodes.definition_zh: 用历史数据检验一套交易策略过去的表现。常见陷阱是未来函数(look-ahead bias)——不慎用到了当时本无法获得的信息,从而美化了结果。

nodes.definition_en: Practicing a strategy with simulated money in real market conditions, without risking actual capital. It's a testing ground before going to real trading (实盘, live trading).
nodes.definition_zh: 在真实市场行情下用模拟资金演练策略,不投入实际本金。它是转向实盘(live trading)之前的试验场。

nodes.definition_en: Studying price and volume charts to guess where a market is headed, on the belief that patterns repeat. Common tools include candlestick charts (K线) and moving averages (均线, MA).
nodes.definition_zh: 通过研究价格和成交量图表来推测市场走向,其信念是形态会重复出现。常用工具包括K线(candlestick)和均线(moving average, MA)。

nodes.definition_en: Valuing an investment by studying the underlying business and economy — earnings, growth, competitive position, financial health — rather than price charts, to judge what it's really worth.
nodes.definition_zh: 通过研究背后的企业与经济基本面——盈利、增长、竞争地位、财务健康——而非价格图表,来判断一项投资的真实价值。

nodes.definition_en: Price levels where a stock has repeatedly stopped falling (support) or stopped rising (resistance), as buyers or sellers reliably step in. Chart-watchers treat them as floors and ceilings that may hold or break.
nodes.definition_zh: 股票反复止跌(支撑位)或止涨(压力位)的价格水平,买方或卖方在此稳定入场。看图者把它们视作可能守住或被突破的地板与天花板。

nodes.definition_en: An ownership stake in a company. Holding equity (a share) makes you a part-owner, entitled to a slice of profits and, usually, a vote — and exposed to the full ups and downs of the business.
nodes.definition_zh: 对一家公司的所有权份额。持有股票(权益)即成为部分所有者,有权分享部分利润、通常还有投票权——同时也承担企业全部的涨跌。

nodes.definition_en: Two share types. Common stock carries voting rights and full exposure to the company's fortunes. Preferred stock usually pays a fixed dividend and ranks ahead of common in a payout, but typically has no vote — a hybrid between stock and bond.
nodes.definition_zh: 两类股份。普通股有投票权,完整承担公司的兴衰。优先股通常派发固定股息,分配顺序优先于普通股,但一般没有投票权——是介于股票与债券之间的混合品。

nodes.definition_en: A certificate traded on a US exchange that represents shares of a foreign company, letting Americans invest in it as easily as a domestic stock. ADRs are the main way most US-listed Chinese companies (中概股) trade.
nodes.definition_zh: 在美国交易所挂牌、代表某外国公司股份的凭证,让美国投资者能像买本土股票一样投资它。ADR 是多数在美上市中概股的主要交易形式。

nodes.definition_en: A loan you make to a government or company in exchange for regular interest payments and repayment of the principal at a set date. Unlike a shareholder, a bondholder is a lender, not an owner.
nodes.definition_zh: 你借给政府或公司的一笔贷款,换取定期利息以及在约定日期偿还本金。与股东不同,债券持有人是债权人,而非所有者。

nodes.definition_en: A bond issued by a national government, generally seen as one of the safest investments because the state stands behind it.
nodes.definition_zh: 由国家政府发行的债券,通常被视为最安全的投资之一,因为有国家信用背书。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Same word, different referent. In English, 'Treasuries' almost always means US Treasury bonds — the global benchmark safe asset. In Chinese, 国债 generically means government bonds and, in a domestic context, refers to Chinese government bonds (CGBs). They are different issuers with different yields and roles, so 'Treasury yield' can mean the US or the Chinese one depending on which market you're in.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 同一个词,所指不同。英文里「Treasuries」几乎总指美国国债——全球基准安全资产。中文的「国债」泛指政府债券,在国内语境下指中国国债(CGB)。两者是不同发行主体,收益率和角色都不同,因此「国债收益率」到底指美国还是中国,取决于你身处哪个市场。

nodes.definition_en: The return an investment produces, expressed as a percentage. For a bond, the key measure is yield to maturity (YTM) — the total annual return you'd earn holding it to the end, given its price, interest, and repayment.
nodes.definition_zh: 一项投资产生的回报,以百分比表示。对债券而言,核心指标是到期收益率(YTM)——考虑价格、利息和到期偿还后,持有至到期每年可获得的总回报。

nodes.definition_en: The fixed interest a bond pays its holder, usually stated as an annual percentage of the bond's face value. A 5% coupon on a 100 bond pays 5 a year.
nodes.definition_zh: 债券向持有人支付的固定利息,通常以债券面值的年百分比表示。面值100、票息5%的债券,每年支付5。

nodes.definition_en: A bond that carries real risk the issuer might not repay — typically issued by a company rather than a government — so it pays extra yield to compensate for that credit risk.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种存在发行人可能违约风险的债券——通常由企业而非政府发行——因此支付更高的收益率来补偿这份信用风险。
nodes.nuance_note_en: China's bond market is commonly split into a distinctive binary: 信用债 (credit bonds, with meaningful default risk) versus 利率债 (rate bonds — government and policy-bank bonds treated as effectively risk-free). US fixed income doesn't use this exact two-way frame; it categorizes by issuer type (Treasuries, agencies, corporates, munis) instead, so the clean 信用债/利率债 dividing line is a Chinese-market convention.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 中国债券市场常按一种独特的二分法划分:信用债(有实质违约风险)与利率债(国债及政策性金融债,被视作近乎无风险)。美国固收并不使用这种确切的两分框架,而是按发行主体分类(国债、机构债、公司债、市政债),因此干净的信用债/利率债之分是中国市场的特有惯例。

nodes.definition_en: A bond that can be converted into a set number of the issuer's shares. It pays interest like a bond but can rise with the stock, offering a mix of downside protection and equity upside.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种可按约定比例转换为发行人股票的债券。它像债券一样付息,又能随股价上涨,兼具下行保护与股权上行空间。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Convertibles exist in both markets, but China's 可转债 have distinctive features. They're a favorite of retail investors, trade T+0 (buy and sell same day, unlike A-share stocks' T+1), and have their own price-limit and forced-conversion rules. So the trading behavior and rules around a Chinese convertible differ noticeably from a typical US convertible, which is largely an institutional instrument.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 可转债两地都有,但中国的可转债别具特色。它是散户偏爱的品种,实行 T+0(当日可买卖,不同于A股股票的 T+1),并有自己的涨跌幅和强制赎回规则。因此中国可转债的交易行为与规则,与主要面向机构的美式可转债有明显不同。

nodes.definition_en: A bond from a lower-rated, riskier issuer that pays a high interest rate to compensate for the greater chance of default. 'Junk' is the blunt nickname for below-investment-grade debt.
nodes.definition_zh: 由评级较低、风险较高的发行人发行的债券,以高利率补偿更大的违约概率。「垃圾债」是对投资级以下债务的直白俗称。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of how sensitive a bond's price is to interest-rate changes. Longer duration means bigger price swings when rates move — a rough gauge of a bond's interest-rate risk.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量债券价格对利率变化敏感程度的指标。久期越长,利率变动时价格波动越大——它粗略反映债券的利率风险。

nodes.definition_en: The extra yield a risky bond pays over a safe government bond of the same maturity — the market's price for taking on the issuer's default risk. A widening spread signals rising worry about defaults.
nodes.definition_zh: 一只风险债券相对同期限安全国债多支付的收益率——即市场为承担发行人违约风险所要的补偿。利差走阔意味着对违约的担忧上升。

nodes.definition_en: When a borrower fails to make a promised payment on a debt — missing interest or failing to repay principal. In China, defaults on LGFV bonds (城投债) are a particularly watched theme.
nodes.definition_zh: 借款人未能按约定偿付债务——未付利息或未还本金。在中国,城投债(LGFV bond)的违约是尤为受关注的主题。

nodes.definition_en: A basic raw material or agricultural product that is interchangeable and traded in bulk — like crude oil, gold, copper, or soybeans. Because one unit is much like another, price is set by global supply and demand.
nodes.definition_zh: 可互相替代、大批量交易的基础原材料或农产品——如原油、黄金、铜、大豆。由于每单位大同小异,价格由全球供需决定。

nodes.definition_en: The main US exchange for trading metals futures — gold, silver, and copper in particular — and a key venue for setting global metals prices.
nodes.definition_zh: 美国交易金属期货(尤其是黄金、白银、铜)的主要交易所,也是全球金属定价的关键场所。

nodes.definition_en: The main US exchange for energy futures, most notably WTI crude oil and natural gas — a central reference point for world energy prices.
nodes.definition_zh: 美国交易能源期货的主要交易所,尤以 WTI 原油和天然气著称,是世界能源价格的重要参考。

nodes.definition_en: The London Metal Exchange, the world's main pricing hub for industrial base metals such as aluminum, copper, zinc, and nickel.
nodes.definition_zh: 伦敦金属交易所,全球有色金属(如铝、铜、锌、镍)的主要定价中心。

nodes.definition_en: The Chicago Board of Trade, a major exchange especially for agricultural futures like corn, wheat, and soybeans.
nodes.definition_zh: 芝加哥期货交易所,尤其在玉米、小麦、大豆等农产品期货方面举足轻重的交易所。

nodes.zh: 上期所 / 大商所 / 郑商所
nodes.definition_en: China's three main commodity futures exchanges: the Shanghai Futures Exchange (metals, energy), the Dalian Commodity Exchange (agriculture, iron ore), and the Zhengzhou Commodity Exchange (agriculture, chemicals).
nodes.definition_zh: 中国三大商品期货交易所:上海期货交易所(金属、能源)、大连商品交易所(农产品、铁矿石)、郑州商品交易所(农产品、化工品)。

nodes.definition_en: The world's two main crude-oil price benchmarks: WTI (West Texas Intermediate) references US oil, while Brent references North Sea oil and is the wider international standard. Most oil trades are priced off one of the two.
nodes.definition_zh: 全球两大原油价格基准:WTI(西德州中质原油)以美国原油为参照,布伦特以北海原油为参照、是更广泛的国际标准。多数原油交易以其中之一定价。

nodes.definition_en: The price to buy or sell gold for immediate delivery, as opposed to a futures price for later. The London spot market (伦敦金) is the main global reference.
nodes.definition_zh: 即时交割黄金的买卖价格,区别于约定未来交割的期货价。伦敦现货市场(伦敦金)是全球主要参照。

nodes.definition_en: Two shapes of the futures curve. Contango is when longer-dated futures cost more than the spot price (a forward premium); backwardation is when they cost less (a forward discount). The shape affects the return from rolling futures over time.
nodes.definition_zh: 期货曲线的两种形态。升水(contango)指更远期的期货价格高于现货(远期溢价);贴水(backwardation)指低于现货(现货溢价)。这一形态影响滚动持有期货的收益。

nodes.definition_en: The gain or loss you get from replacing an expiring futures contract with a later-dated one. It's a key driver of returns for commodity-index investors, since they must keep rolling positions forward.
nodes.definition_zh: 用较远月合约替换即将到期合约时产生的盈亏。由于商品指数投资者必须不断向后展期,它是其收益的关键来源。

nodes.definition_en: A fund that pools money from many ordinary investors and hires a manager to invest it in a portfolio of stocks or bonds. Open to the general public and regulated for retail investors.
nodes.definition_zh: 把众多普通投资者的资金集合起来、聘请管理人投资于股票或债券组合的基金。面向公众开放,并受面向散户的监管。

nodes.definition_en: A fund raised privately from a limited number of qualified investors rather than the general public, with lighter disclosure and more freedom in what it can invest in.
nodes.definition_zh: 面向有限数量的合格投资者私下募集、而非向公众公开发行的基金,披露要求较松,投资范围也更自由。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Watch the false friend: Chinese 私募基金 does not equal US 'private equity'. 私募基金 is a broad umbrella for privately-raised funds of all kinds — hedge funds, quant funds, and equity funds alike (a quant 私募 is essentially a hedge fund). US 'private equity' means specifically funds that buy and restructure whole companies. Translating 私募 as 'private equity' narrows a broad category to one slice of it.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 注意这个「假朋友」:中文的私募基金并不等于美式「private equity」。私募基金是各类私下募集基金的统称——对冲、量化、股权都涵盖在内(量化私募本质就是对冲基金)。而美式「private equity」专指收购并重组整家公司的基金。把私募译成 private equity,会把一个宽泛范畴窄化为其中一小块。

nodes.definition_en: A privately-run fund for wealthy or institutional investors that uses flexible, aggressive strategies — shorting, leverage, derivatives — to seek returns in any market direction, typically charging high fees.
nodes.definition_zh: 面向富裕或机构投资者的私募型基金,运用做空、杠杆、衍生品等灵活激进的策略,在任何市场方向上追求收益,通常收取高额费用。

nodes.zh: 交易型开放式指数基金
nodes.definition_en: A fund that trades on an exchange like a single stock, usually tracking an index. It offers the diversification of a fund with the ease of buying and selling a share throughout the day.
nodes.definition_zh: 像单只股票一样在交易所买卖的基金,通常跟踪某一指数。它兼具基金的分散性和股票般随时买卖的便利。

nodes.definition_en: A fund that simply mirrors a market index rather than trying to beat it — a passive strategy. Because it's not actively stock-picking, it usually charges much lower fees than an active fund.
nodes.definition_zh: 只复制某一市场指数、而不试图跑赢它的基金——一种被动策略。由于不主动选股,费用通常远低于主动基金。

nodes.definition_en: The per-unit value of a fund — its total assets minus liabilities, divided by the number of units. It's the price at which you buy into or redeem from most open-end funds, and the core number for tracking performance.
nodes.definition_zh: 基金每一份额的价值——总资产减负债,再除以份额数。它是申购、赎回多数开放式基金的价格,也是跟踪业绩的核心数字。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Chinese fund reporting quotes two NAV figures side by side: 单位净值 (unit NAV, the current per-unit value) and 累计净值 (cumulative NAV, which adds back all historical distributions to show total value created since inception). US funds typically report a single NAV plus total return separately, so a Chinese reader comparing 累计净值 with a US NAV is not comparing like with like.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 中国基金披露会并列两个净值:单位净值(当前每份价值)和累计净值(把历史分红加回,反映成立以来创造的总价值)。美国基金通常只报单一净值,再另行给出总回报,因此拿累计净值去对比美式 NAV,并非同口径比较。

nodes.definition_en: The total market value of all the money a fund or manager oversees on behalf of clients. A common measure of a firm's size and, roughly, its fee-earning power.
nodes.definition_zh: 一只基金或一位管理人代客户管理的全部资金的市场总值。它是衡量机构规模、并大致反映其收费能力的常用指标。

nodes.definition_en: The ongoing cost of owning a fund, charged as a yearly percentage of your investment. It quietly reduces your return every year, so lower is generally better. Subscription and redemption fees may apply when you buy or sell.
nodes.definition_zh: 持有一只基金的持续成本,按投资额的年百分比收取。它每年都会悄悄减少你的回报,因此通常越低越好。买卖时还可能收取申购、赎回费。

nodes.definition_en: How you enter and exit an open-end fund: subscription is buying units, redemption is selling them back to the fund at its net asset value. (A closed-end fund, by contrast, trades a fixed number of units on an exchange.)
nodes.definition_zh: 进出开放式基金的方式:申购是买入份额,赎回是按净值把份额卖回给基金。(相比之下,封闭式基金的份额数量固定,在交易所买卖。)

nodes.definition_en: A sales fee on a fund charged either when you buy in (front-end load) or when you sell out (back-end load). It's separate from the fund's ongoing management fee.
nodes.definition_zh: 基金的销售费用,可在买入时收取(前端收费)或在卖出时收取(后端收费)。它独立于基金的持续管理费。

nodes.definition_en: How closely a fund follows the index it's meant to track. A small tracking error means the fund hugs its benchmark tightly; a large one means it drifts away — a key quality measure for index funds.
nodes.definition_zh: 一只基金跟随其目标指数的贴合程度。跟踪误差小说明紧贴基准;大则说明偏离——这是评价指数基金的关键指标。

nodes.definition_en: How long a fund's current manager has been running it. A longer track record gives investors more history to judge, and a change of manager can meaningfully alter a fund's approach.
nodes.definition_zh: 基金现任经理执掌该基金的时长。任职越久,投资者可据以判断的历史越充分;更换经理则可能实质改变基金的风格。

nodes.definition_en: When a fund gradually strays from its stated investment style — say, a value fund creeping into growth stocks. It matters because investors picked the fund for a specific role in their portfolio.
nodes.definition_zh: 基金逐渐偏离其声称的投资风格——例如价值型基金悄悄买入成长股。这很重要,因为投资者当初正是看中它在组合中的特定角色。

nodes.definition_en: The classic hedge-fund and private-fund fee model: a 2% annual management fee on assets plus 20% of any profits (the performance fee, or carry). The manager earns both for running the money and for making it grow.
nodes.definition_zh: 对冲基金和私募的经典收费模式:按资产每年收取2%管理费,再加上利润的20%(业绩报酬,carry)。管理人既因管理资金、也因让资金增值而获得回报。

nodes.definition_en: The highest value a fund has previously reached, used as a threshold for performance fees. The manager only earns a performance fee on gains above this mark, so investors aren't charged twice for recovering past losses.
nodes.definition_zh: 基金此前达到过的最高值,用作计提业绩报酬的门槛。管理人只有在超过这个水位的收益上才收取业绩报酬,以免投资者为弥补过往亏损而被重复收费。

nodes.definition_en: A stretch of time during which investors cannot withdraw money from a fund. It lets the manager invest without worrying about sudden redemptions, but ties up your capital until it ends.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资者不能从基金赎回资金的一段时期。它让管理人无需担心突发赎回而安心投资,但也把你的资金锁定到期满为止。

nodes.definition_en: A fund that invests in other funds rather than directly in stocks or bonds, aiming to spread risk across managers and strategies. The trade-off is paying two layers of fees.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资于其他基金、而非直接投资股票或债券的基金,目的是在多个管理人和策略间分散风险。代价是要承担两层费用。

nodes.definition_en: A fund that pools investors' money to own income-producing property or infrastructure and pays out most of the rental income as dividends, letting ordinary investors hold real-estate-like assets without buying buildings directly.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种把投资者资金集合起来、持有能产生租金收入的不动产或基础设施的基金,并将大部分租金以分红形式派发,使普通投资者无需直接买楼即可持有类似房产的资产。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Same instrument, different contents. US REITs are dominated by commercial real estate — offices, malls, apartments, warehouses. China's public REITs only launched in 2021 and are built mainly around infrastructure such as highways, industrial parks, and utilities rather than commercial property. So 'REITs' in the two markets wraps quite different underlying assets even though the structure is the same.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 同一种工具,装的东西不同。美国REITs以商业地产为主——写字楼、购物中心、公寓、仓储。中国公募REITs 2021年才起步,底层主要是高速公路、产业园、公用事业等基础设施,而非商业地产。因此两地的「REITs」外壳相同,包裹的底层资产却相当不一样。

nodes.definition_en: Investing in companies that aren't publicly traded — buying stakes in private firms, often to grow or restructure them and sell later at a profit. This happens in the primary market (一级市场), where shares are bought directly rather than on an exchange.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资于未上市公司——买入私人企业的股份,通常为培育或重组后择机高价退出。这发生在一级市场(primary market),即直接买入股份而非在交易所买卖。

nodes.definition_en: A form of private investment that funds young, high-growth startups in exchange for equity, accepting a high failure rate in the hope that a few big winners more than pay for the losses.
nodes.definition_zh: 私人投资的一种,向年轻的高成长初创企业注资以换取股权,接受较高的失败率,寄望少数大赢家足以覆盖亏损而有余。

nodes.zh: 一级市场 / 二级市场
nodes.definition_en: The primary market is where securities are first created and sold — an IPO or a private placement. The secondary market is where existing securities then trade among investors, like a stock exchange. In Chinese, '一二级' is everyday shorthand for this pair.
nodes.definition_zh: 一级市场是证券首次产生并出售的地方——如IPO或定增。二级市场是已有证券在投资者之间交易的地方,如股票交易所。中文里「一二级」是对这对概念的日常简称。

nodes.definition_en: Digital assets like Bitcoin that run on blockchain networks rather than being issued by any government, and can be traded or held as speculative investments.
nodes.definition_zh: 如比特币这类运行在区块链网络上、并非由任何政府发行的数字资产,可作为投机性投资进行交易或持有。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The asset itself is the same worldwide, but its legal status is not. In the US, crypto can be traded and held legally (within a developing regulatory framework). In mainland China, crypto trading is banned and the topic is compliance-sensitive, so the same asset that's an ordinary investment in the US is off-limits in the mainland.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 资产本身全球相同,但法律地位不同。在美国,加密资产可合法交易和持有(监管框架仍在发展中)。在中国大陆,加密交易被禁止,该话题在合规上敏感,因此同一种资产在美国是普通投资,在大陆却被禁止。

nodes.definition_en: A pre-packaged investment built from derivatives that delivers a custom payoff — for example, an above-market yield as long as an index stays within a range, with losses if it falls too far.
nodes.definition_zh: 由衍生品搭建、提供定制化收益结构的打包投资产品——例如只要指数维持在某区间内就给出高于市场的收益,但一旦跌得过深便产生亏损。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Structured products exist globally, but China has a distinctive, hugely popular local variant: the 雪球 ('snowball') autocallable. It offers steady coupons while an index stays above a floor, but exposes investors to sharp losses if the index breaks down — a payoff that drew heavy retail money and specific regulatory attention in China in a way its global cousins didn't.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 结构化产品全球都有,但中国有一个独特且极受欢迎的本土变体:雪球(snowball autocallable)。只要指数维持在敲入线之上就持续派息,一旦指数跌破便让投资者承受大幅亏损——这种结构在中国吸引了大量散户资金、并引来专门的监管关注,是其海外同类所没有的。

nodes.definition_en: A very low-risk fund that holds short-term, high-quality debt and aims to keep a stable value while paying modest interest — used as a cash-like place to park money. China's Yu'e Bao (余额宝) is a well-known example.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种极低风险的基金,持有短期、高质量的债务工具,力求价值稳定并支付温和利息——用作类现金的资金停泊处。中国的余额宝即是著名例子。

nodes.definition_en: In China, an investment product sold by banks to retail customers as a higher-yielding alternative to a deposit, pooling money into bonds and other assets.
nodes.definition_zh: 在中国,由银行向零售客户销售、作为存款的更高收益替代品的投资产品,把资金投向债券等资产。
nodes.nuance_note_en: This is a China-specific asset-management category with no clean US counterpart. Bank WMPs (银行理财) grew huge partly because they were long seen as carrying an implicit guarantee; the 2018 asset-management rules (资管新规) forced them onto a mark-to-market (net-value) basis, ending that assumed protection. US banks sell funds and CDs, but nothing occupies the same quasi-deposit, bank-branded role at this scale, so there's no direct equivalent.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这是中国特有的资管品类,美国没有干净的对应物。银行理财之所以规模庞大,部分因为长期被视为存在刚性兑付;2018年的资管新规迫使其转为净值化(按市值计价),终结了这种默认保护。美国银行也卖基金和存单,但没有任何产品在如此规模上扮演同样的「准存款、银行品牌」角色,故无直接对应。

nodes.definition_en: An organized marketplace where stocks and other securities are bought and sold under common rules. Major examples include the Shanghai (SSE), Shenzhen (SZSE), Beijing (BSE), and Hong Kong (HKEX) exchanges, alongside the NYSE and Nasdaq.
nodes.definition_zh: 在统一规则下买卖股票及其他证券的有组织市场。主要例子有上交所(SSE)、深交所(SZSE)、北交所(BSE)、港交所(HKEX),以及纽交所(NYSE)和纳斯达克(NASDAQ)。

nodes.zh: 板块(主板/创业板/科创板)
nodes.definition_en: A tier of a stock market with its own listing standards and rules. China's exchanges have several: the Main Board for established firms, ChiNext (创业板) and the STAR Market (科创板) for growth and tech companies.
nodes.definition_zh: 股票市场中拥有自身上市标准和规则的层级。中国交易所设有多个板块:面向成熟企业的主板,以及面向成长与科技企业的创业板(ChiNext)和科创板(STAR Market)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The concept of market tiers exists everywhere, but China's boards are a distinctive multi-board structure where the board you list on changes concrete rules — different price-limit bands (±20% on ChiNext and STAR vs ±10% on the Main Board), listing thresholds, and investor-eligibility requirements. US markets have exchanges and tiers, but not this same board-by-board rulebook, so 'board' carries more regulatory weight in China.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 市场分层的概念各处都有,但中国的板块是一种独特的多板结构,上市在哪个板会改变具体规则——不同的涨跌幅(创业板、科创板±20%,主板±10%)、上市门槛和投资者适当性要求。美国市场也有交易所和分层,却没有这种逐板设定规则的体系,因此「板块」在中国承载更重的监管含义。

nodes.definition_en: A single number that tracks the combined performance of a basket of stocks, used to gauge how a market or segment is doing. Examples include the SSE Composite, CSI 300, CSI 500, CSI 1000, and Hang Seng indices.
nodes.definition_zh: 用一个数字追踪一篮子股票的综合表现,用来衡量某个市场或板块的走势。例子包括上证指数、沪深300、中证500、中证1000和恒生指数。

nodes.zh: 标普500 / 纳指 / 道指
nodes.definition_en: The three headline US stock indices: the S&P 500 tracks 500 large companies (the broad benchmark), the Nasdaq is tech-heavy, and the Dow follows 30 blue-chip firms.
nodes.definition_zh: 美国三大标志性股指:标普500追踪500家大型公司(宽基基准),纳指(纳斯达克)以科技股为主,道指(道琼斯)追踪30家蓝筹公司。

nodes.definition_en: A large, well-established, financially solid company whose stock is considered relatively safe and reliable.
nodes.definition_zh: 规模大、根基稳、财务稳健的公司,其股票被视为相对安全可靠。
nodes.nuance_note_en: 'Blue chip' maps closely to 蓝筹股, but Chinese markets add a second term with no exact English equivalent: 白马股 ('white horse'). It refers to well-known, high-quality companies with proven, steady earnings and transparent operations — overlapping with blue chips but carrying a specific flavor of a dependable, widely-followed performer. There's no single English word that captures 白马 precisely.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 「blue chip」与蓝筹股基本对应,但中文市场还有一个英文没有确切对应词的术语:白马股。它指知名度高、盈利稳定且经营透明的优质公司——与蓝筹有重叠,但带有「稳健、被广泛跟踪的绩优标的」这一特定意味。英文没有单一词能精确对应「白马」。

nodes.definition_en: A company with a relatively small market value. Small caps tend to be more volatile and less liquid than large ones, but can offer faster growth. Stocks are commonly grouped as large, mid, and small cap (大盘/中盘/小盘).
nodes.definition_zh: 市值相对较小的公司。小盘股通常比大盘股波动更大、流动性更差,但可能增长更快。股票常按大盘、中盘、小盘(large/mid/small cap)分类。

nodes.definition_en: Two broad investing styles. Growth stocks are companies expected to expand quickly, priced high on future potential; value stocks look cheap relative to current earnings or assets, bought in hope the market has underrated them.
nodes.definition_zh: 两种主要投资风格。成长股是预期快速扩张的公司,因看重未来潜力而估值偏高;价值股相对当前盈利或资产显得便宜,买入是寄望市场低估了它们。

nodes.definition_en: A grouping of companies that do similar business — such as banks, semiconductors, or consumer goods — used to compare peers and track how parts of the market move.
nodes.definition_zh: 把从事相似业务的公司归为一类——如银行、半导体、消费品——用来对比同行并跟踪市场各部分的走势。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The concept is the same, but the standard classification differs. A-share analysis uses the Shenwan (申万, SWS) industry classification as its default taxonomy, while US markets typically use GICS. Because the two systems draw industry boundaries differently, a company can sit in different 'sectors' depending on which scheme you're reading, so cross-market sector comparisons need a mapping.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 概念相同,但标准分类不同。A股分析以申万(SWS)行业分类为默认体系,美国市场通常用 GICS。由于两套体系划分行业边界的方式不同,同一家公司在不同体系下可能落入不同「行业」,因此跨市场的行业对比需要做映射。

nodes.definition_en: A bull market is a sustained period of rising prices and optimism; a bear market is a sustained decline, often defined as a drop of 20% or more. A sideways, directionless market is called range-bound or choppy (震荡市).
nodes.definition_zh: 牛市是价格持续上涨、情绪乐观的时期;熊市是持续下跌,常以跌幅达20%或以上为界。横盘、无明确方向的市场称为震荡市(range-bound/choppy)。

nodes.zh: 反弹 / 回调 / 崩盘
nodes.definition_en: Words for market moves by scale: a rally is a notable rise, a correction is a moderate pullback (often around 10%), and a crash is a sudden, severe collapse in prices.
nodes.definition_zh: 按幅度描述市场走势的词:反弹是明显上涨,回调是温和回落(常在10%左右),崩盘是价格突然剧烈的暴跌。

nodes.definition_en: An automatic, temporary halt to trading triggered when prices fall too fast, meant to pause panic and let markets cool down.
nodes.definition_zh: 当价格下跌过快时自动触发的临时停牌机制,意在暂停恐慌、让市场冷静。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The US has working market-wide circuit breakers that pause trading at set decline thresholds. China's experience is different: it introduced a circuit-breaker system in January 2016, but it backfired — halts fed the selling — and it was scrapped after just four trading days. So in the US it's a standing safeguard, while in China 熔断 mainly refers to that failed 2016 episode, and A-shares now rely on daily per-stock price limits instead.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国有正常运作的全市场熔断,在设定的跌幅阈值暂停交易。中国的经历不同:2016年1月引入熔断机制,却适得其反——停牌反而加剧抛售——仅四个交易日便被叫停。因此在美国它是常设的安全阀,在中国「熔断」主要指2016年那次失败的尝试,如今A股改以每日个股涨跌停来替代。

nodes.definition_en: The first time a private company sells its shares to the public and lists on a stock exchange, turning it into a publicly-traded company and raising money in the process.
nodes.definition_zh: 一家私人公司首次向公众发售股份并在交易所挂牌,从而变成上市公司,并在过程中募集资金。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Going public means the same thing, but the approval regime differs. China historically gated IPOs through an approval-based system (核准制) where a regulator decided who could list and influenced pricing and timing; it has been shifting to a registration-based system (注册制) closer to the US disclosure-based model. So an A-share IPO has historically involved more official gatekeeping around eligibility and pricing than a typical US listing.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 上市这件事含义相同,但审批制度不同。中国过去通过核准制来管控IPO,由监管决定谁能上市,并影响定价与节奏;现正转向更接近美式披露制的注册制。因此A股IPO在准入和定价上,历来比典型的美股上市有更多官方把关。

nodes.definition_en: When a company's shares are removed from an exchange and can no longer be traded there — because it fails to meet listing standards, goes bankrupt, or is taken private.
nodes.definition_zh: 公司股份被从交易所摘牌、不再能在该所交易——原因可能是不满足上市标准、破产或私有化。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Delisting exists in both markets, but A-shares wrap it in a distinctive warning system. Companies at risk are flagged with an ST or *ST prefix (退市风险警示) before removal, giving investors a formal red flag. Historically delisting was rare in China and the ST label became a closely-watched risk signal — machinery that has no direct US counterpart.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 退市两地都有,但A股为它配了一套独特的警示系统。有退市风险的公司在摘牌前会被冠以 ST 或 *ST 前缀(退市风险警示),给投资者一个正式的红旗。历史上中国退市较为罕见,ST 标签也因此成为被密切关注的风险信号——这套机制在美国并无直接对应。

nodes.zh: 北向资金 / 南向资金
nodes.definition_en: Money moving through the Stock Connect links between mainland China and Hong Kong: northbound flow is foreign money buying mainland A-shares, southbound flow is mainland money buying Hong Kong stocks.
nodes.definition_zh: 通过沪深港通在中国大陆与香港之间流动的资金:北向资金是外资买入大陆A股,南向资金是内地资金买入港股。
nodes.nuance_note_en: No US equivalent. These flows are a feature of the specific Stock Connect plumbing between the mainland and Hong Kong, built to let capital cross a partially-closed border. Northbound flow in particular is watched as a real-time gauge of foreign sentiment toward A-shares. Because the US has no equivalent controlled cross-border channel, there's no comparable 'northbound/southbound' metric.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国无对应。这些流向是沪深港通这一特定通道的产物,专为让资金跨越部分封闭的边界而设。北向资金尤其被当作外资对A股情绪的实时风向标。由于美国没有类似的受控跨境通道,也就没有可比的「北向/南向」指标。

nodes.definition_en: The price gap for companies that are listed both in mainland China (A-shares) and Hong Kong (H-shares). Since the same company's two share classes often trade at different prices, the premium measures how much more (or less) the A-share costs than the H-share.
nodes.definition_zh: 同时在中国大陆(A股)和香港(H股)上市的公司,其两地股价之间的差距。由于同一家公司的两类股份常以不同价格交易,溢价衡量A股比H股贵(或便宜)多少。
nodes.nuance_note_en: No US equivalent. The A/H premium exists only because of the dual-listing structure and the segmented, capital-controlled markets that keep A-shares and H-shares from converging in price. US-listed companies don't have a parallel domestic-versus-offshore share class trading at a persistent gap, so there is no comparable premium to track.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国无对应。A/H溢价之所以存在,完全是因为这种两地上市结构,以及被资本管制分割、使A股与H股价格无法趋同的市场。美国上市公司没有类似的「境内 vs 离岸」两类股份长期存在价差,因此没有可比的溢价可供跟踪。

nodes.definition_en: The two broad groups of market participants: institutions are professional outfits investing large pools (funds, insurers, pensions), while retail investors are individuals trading their own money.
nodes.definition_zh: 市场参与者的两大类:机构是管理大额资金的专业机构(基金、保险、养老金),散户则是用自有资金交易的个人。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The basic split is universal, but the balance and vocabulary differ. A-share turnover is far more retail-driven than the institution-dominated US market, and Chinese usage adds actor categories with no strict English equivalent — notably 游资 ('hot money', fast-moving speculative capital) and 主力 ('main force', the big players presumed to be steering a stock). These are market slang rather than defined institutional types.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 基本划分是通用的,但结构和词汇不同。A股成交远比以机构为主的美国市场更由散户驱动,且中文还有英文无严格对应的角色类别——尤其是游资(hot money,快速进出的投机资金)和主力(main force,被认为在主导某只股票的大玩家)。它们更像市场黑话,而非有明确定义的机构类型。

nodes.definition_en: A gauge of how broadly a market move is shared across stocks — for instance, how many stocks are rising versus falling. Strong breadth means a rally is widespread rather than driven by just a few big names.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量一次市场变动在多大范围内被各股票共享的指标——例如上涨与下跌家数的对比。宽度强意味着上涨是普遍的,而非仅由少数大权重股拉动。

nodes.definition_en: The overall mood of investors — how optimistic or fearful they are — which can drive prices in the short term regardless of fundamentals.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资者的整体情绪——乐观还是恐惧——它在短期内可以不顾基本面而推动价格。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Sentiment is a universal idea, but the customary proxies used to measure it differ. In China, common gauges are margin-financing balances (两融余额), turnover rate, and northbound inflows. US market-watchers lean on different measures like the VIX, put/call ratios, and investor surveys.
