So reading 'sentiment' in each market means looking at a different dashboard of indicators.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 情绪是通用概念,但衡量它的惯用代理不同。在中国,常用的风向标是两融余额、换手率和北向流入。美国的观察者更依赖 VIX、认沽认购比、投资者调查等不同指标。因此在两地解读「情绪」,看的是各自不同的一套指标盘面。

nodes.definition_en: Where a current valuation sits within its own history, expressed as a percentile. Saying a stock's P/E is 'at the 80th percentile' means it's more expensive than it has been 80% of the time in the past.
nodes.definition_zh: 当前估值处在其自身历史区间中的位置,以分位数表示。说某股票市盈率「处于历史80%分位」,意为它比过去80%的时间都要贵。

nodes.definition_en: Two sides of the investment industry. The sell-side (brokerage research arms, 券商研究所) produces analysis and sells services to investors; the buy-side (funds, asset managers) uses that research to actually invest money.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资行业的两侧。卖方(券商研究所)生产分析、向投资者提供服务;买方(基金、资管机构)则用这些研究去实际投资。

nodes.definition_en: An analyst's summary recommendation on a stock, usually on a scale from most positive to most negative — in China typically 买入 / 增持 / 中性 / 减持 (buy / add / neutral / reduce).
nodes.definition_zh: 分析师对一只股票的总结性建议,通常按从最看好到最看空的等级排列——中国常见为买入 / 增持 / 中性 / 减持。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Rating scales exist in both markets but don't map one-to-one. The Chinese ladder (买入/增持/中性/减持) is often rendered as buy/overweight/neutral/underweight, but 增持 and 减持 literally mean 'add to' and 'reduce' holdings, which isn't quite the portfolio-weighting sense of 'overweight/underweight'. The rough correspondence hides a slight difference in what the middle ratings actually say.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 评级体系两地都有,却并非一一对应。中文的买入/增持/中性/减持常被译作 buy/overweight/neutral/underweight,但「增持」「减持」字面是「增加/减少持仓」,与「overweight/underweight」那种组合配置含义并不完全相同。这种大致对应掩盖了中间评级在措辞上的细微差别。

nodes.definition_en: An analyst's estimate of where a stock's price should be over a set horizon, usually the next 12 months. It pairs with a rating to show both direction and how far the analyst thinks the stock can move.
nodes.definition_zh: 分析师对一只股票在特定期限(通常未来12个月)应达到价格的估计。它与评级搭配,既表明方向,也表明分析师认为股价还能走多远。

nodes.definition_en: The total number of a stock's shares that investors have sold short — borrowed and sold in a bet the price will fall — usually shown as a share count or as a percentage of shares available. A gauge of how heavily the market is betting against a stock.
nodes.definition_zh: 投资者对某只股票已卖空的股份总量——即借入并卖出、押注价格下跌的部分——通常以股数或占流通股的百分比表示,用来衡量市场看空一只股票的程度。
nodes.nuance_note_en: This is a US-market indicator with essentially no A-share counterpart. A meaningful short-interest number needs easy, widespread share-borrowing to exist; in China's A-share market shorting is tightly restricted and securities lending is thin, so no comparable short-interest figure is compiled or watched. The gap runs one way: the metric is standard in the US and largely absent in mainland China.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这是美股市场的指标,在A股几乎没有对应。有意义的空头头寸数据,前提是做空所需的融券便捷且普遍;而A股做空受到严格限制、融券规模很小,因此并不存在可比的空头头寸数据供统计或观察。差异是单向的:该指标在美国很常规,在中国大陆基本缺失。

nodes.definition_en: An index that measures how much volatility investors expect in the US stock market over the next month, read from option prices. It tends to spike when markets are fearful, earning its nickname the 'fear gauge' (恐慌指数).
nodes.definition_zh: 一个从期权价格反推、衡量投资者对未来一个月美国股市预期波动程度的指数。市场恐慌时它往往飙升,故有「恐慌指数」之称。

nodes.definition_en: A shorthand for the market's mood toward risk. 'Risk-on' means investors are confident and pile into riskier assets like stocks; 'risk-off' means they turn cautious and retreat to safe havens like government bonds.
nodes.definition_zh: 对市场风险情绪的简称。「风险偏好升」(risk-on)指投资者有信心、涌入股票等风险资产;「风险偏好降」(risk-off)指转为谨慎、退守到国债等避险资产。

nodes.definition_en: The total value of all goods and services produced within a country over a period — the broadest single measure of the size of an economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 一个国家在一段时期内生产的全部商品和服务的总价值,是衡量经济体量最综合的单一指标。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of how the average price of a basket of everyday goods and services changes over time — the standard gauge of inflation (通胀). Falling prices instead mean deflation (通缩).
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量一篮子日常商品和服务的平均价格随时间变化的指标——通胀(inflation)的标准衡量。价格下跌则为通缩(deflation)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: CPI works the same way in both countries, but the basket behind it is weighted differently, which changes what the number reflects. China's CPI is heavily influenced by food, especially pork, so it can swing on a single item's supply. US CPI is weighted much more toward housing and services. As a result, the two CPIs respond to different pressures and aren't driven by the same forces.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: CPI 在两国的原理相同,但背后的一篮子权重不同,这改变了数字所反映的内容。中国 CPI 受食品(尤其是猪肉)影响很大,可能因单一品类的供给而波动。美国 CPI 则更偏重住房和服务。因此两地 CPI 回应的压力不同,并非由相同因素驱动。

nodes.zh: 工业生产者出厂价格指数
nodes.definition_en: A measure of the prices producers receive for their goods at the factory gate, before they reach consumers. Because it captures cost pressures early, it's watched as a leading signal for consumer inflation and for industrial-sector profits.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量生产者在出厂环节获得的商品价格(尚未到达消费者)的指标。由于它较早捕捉成本压力,被视作消费端通胀和工业行业利润的先行信号。在中国,PPI 对周期股尤其重要。

nodes.definition_en: A monthly, survey-based gauge of business activity in manufacturing or services, where a reading above 50 signals expansion and below 50 signals contraction. Widely watched as an early read on where the economy is heading.
nodes.definition_zh: 一种基于月度问卷调查的经济活动指标,覆盖制造业或服务业,读数高于50表示扩张、低于50表示收缩,是观察经济走向的重要先行指标。
nodes.nuance_note_en: China publishes two separate PMIs that can point in opposite directions. The official PMI (from the national statistics bureau) surveys a sample tilted toward large state-owned enterprises, while the Caixin PMI leans toward smaller and private firms. Because they cover different slices of the economy, in a given month one can show expansion while the other shows contraction — a split that has no parallel in the single US ISM PMI.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 中国发布两套可能方向相反的PMI。官方PMI(国家统计局)的样本偏向大型国有企业,财新PMI则更偏向中小及民营企业。由于两者覆盖经济的不同切面,某一月份可能一个显示扩张、另一个显示收缩——这种分歧在美国单一的 ISM PMI 中并无对应。

nodes.definition_en: The share of people who want a job and are actively looking but can't find one, out of the total workforce. A core gauge of an economy's health.
nodes.definition_zh: 在全部劳动力中,想工作、正在积极求职却找不到工作的人所占的比例。它是衡量经济健康状况的核心指标。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The two countries measure this very differently. China's headline figure is the surveyed urban unemployment rate (城镇调查失业率), which covers city residents and doesn't fully capture the large migrant-worker and rural workforce. The US rate comes from a household survey covering the whole labor force on a different methodology. So the two numbers aren't measuring the same population and can't be compared at face value.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两国的统计口径差别很大。中国的头条数字是城镇调查失业率,主要覆盖城镇常住人口,并未完全纳入庞大的农民工和农村劳动力。美国的失业率来自覆盖全部劳动力、方法不同的家庭调查。因此两个数字衡量的并非同一人群,不能按面值直接对比。

nodes.definition_en: A closely-watched US monthly report on how many jobs the economy added or lost, excluding farm work. It's one of the biggest scheduled market-moving data releases in the world.
nodes.definition_zh: 备受关注的美国月度报告,统计经济新增或减少的就业人数(不含农业)。它是全球最能撼动市场的定期数据发布之一。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Nonfarm payrolls is a specific US employment release with no Chinese equivalent — China doesn't publish a comparable monthly headcount of jobs added. Despite being a purely US indicator, '非农' is a high-frequency term in Chinese finance media because the release moves global markets. The gap is one-directional: it exists in the US and Chinese traders watch it, but there's no matching Chinese figure.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 非农就业是美国特定的就业数据,中国没有对应指标——中国并不发布可比的月度新增就业人数。尽管是纯美国指标,「非农」仍是中文财经媒体的高频词,因为该数据会牵动全球市场。差异是单向的:它在美国存在、中国交易者也关注,但没有对应的中国数据。

nodes.definition_en: A monthly measure of how much consumers are spending on goods, used to gauge the strength of consumption in the economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量消费者在商品上花费多少的月度指标,用来判断经济中消费的强弱。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Both countries publish a retail-sales figure, but China's 社会消费品零售总额 (社零) is scoped differently from the US Census retail sales series. The Chinese measure includes catering and certain sales to institutions and government units, not just household purchases of goods, so it's a broader tally. Comparing the two headline growth rates without accounting for that scope difference can mislead.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两国都发布零售数据,但中国的社会消费品零售总额(社零)与美国普查局的零售销售口径不同。中式指标涵盖餐饮以及对机关、单位的部分销售,而不仅是居民的商品消费,因此统计范围更宽。不校正这一口径差异就直接对比两地增速,可能产生误导。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of spending on long-lived physical assets across the economy — building factories, roads, housing, and buying equipment. In China it's a headline gauge of investment-led growth.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量全经济体在长期实物资产上的支出——建厂、修路、盖房、购置设备。在中国,它是投资拉动增长的头条指标。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The economic idea — investment in fixed capital — has a clear counterpart, but China's 固投 is measured on a distinctive basis. It's reported monthly on a year-to-date cumulative basis and its scope is broad (including things like land purchases and existing assets that stricter measures exclude). The US tracks investment through separate series like private fixed investment and construction spending, so there's no single US number that lines up cleanly with 固投.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 「固定资本投资」这一经济概念有明确对应,但中国的固投采用独特口径。它按年初至今累计、按月发布,且范围较宽(包含土地购置、存量资产等更严口径会剔除的项目)。美国通过私人固定投资、建筑支出等分开的序列来跟踪投资,因此没有单一美国数字能与固投干净对齐。

nodes.definition_en: A measure of the total output of a country's factories, mines, and utilities — a gauge of how busy the industrial economy is.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量一国工厂、矿山和公用事业总产出的指标——反映工业经济的繁忙程度。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Both countries publish an industrial-output measure, but China's covers only firms 'above designated size' (规模以上) — that is, industrial enterprises above a revenue threshold — rather than all producers. The US industrial production index is built on a different methodology covering the whole sector. So the Chinese figure deliberately excludes smaller firms, and the two series aren't constructed the same way.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两国都发布工业产出指标,但中国的只覆盖「规模以上」企业——即营收超过一定门槛的工业企业——而非全部生产者。美国的工业生产指数则采用覆盖整个行业的不同方法。因此中式数据刻意排除了较小企业,两套序列的编制方式并不相同。

nodes.definition_en: Exports are the goods and services a country sells abroad. The trade balance is exports minus imports: a surplus (顺差) means a country sells more than it buys, a deficit means the reverse.
nodes.definition_zh: 出口是一国向境外销售的商品和服务。贸易差额是出口减进口:顺差意味着卖得比买得多,逆差则相反。

nodes.definition_en: A broad Chinese measure of the total credit and financing flowing from the financial system to the real economy over a period.
nodes.definition_zh: 衡量一定时期内金融体系向实体经济提供的全部资金总量的中国特色指标。
nodes.nuance_note_en: No US equivalent exists. TSF rolls bank loans, bond issuance, and off-balance-sheet financing into one credit gauge the PBOC tracks as a policy target; no single published US figure corresponds to it.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国无对应指标。社融将银行贷款、债券发行、表外融资等合并为一个信用总量,是人民银行的政策观察目标;美国没有单一口径与之对应。

nodes.definition_en: A broad measure of the money supply — cash, checking deposits, and easily-spendable savings — used to gauge how much money is sloshing through the economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 货币供应量的广义口径——现金、活期存款,以及易于动用的储蓄——用来衡量经济中流通的资金规模。
nodes.nuance_note_en: M2 is a standard concept, but exactly which deposits and instruments count toward it differs between China and the US, so the two M2 figures aren't built from the same components. In China, M2 is also paired with total social financing as one of two headline liquidity gauges the market watches together — a companion metric the US doesn't have.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: M2 是通用概念,但具体把哪些存款和工具计入其中,中美并不相同,因此两地的 M2 并非由相同成分构成。在中国,M2 还与社会融资规模并列,作为市场共同关注的两大流动性指标之一——这个搭档指标是美国所没有的。

nodes.definition_en: The stockpile of foreign currencies and other reserve assets a country's central bank holds, used to back its currency, settle international payments, and cushion against financial shocks.
nodes.definition_zh: 一国央行持有的外币及其他储备资产的总量,用于支撑本币、结算国际收支,并缓冲金融冲击。

nodes.definition_en: The exchange rate of the Chinese yuan against the US dollar, which trades in two separate markets: the onshore rate (CNY, inside the mainland) and the offshore rate (CNH, mainly in Hong Kong).
nodes.definition_zh: 人民币兑美元的汇率,它在两个分开的市场交易:在岸汇率(CNY,境内)和离岸汇率(CNH,主要在香港)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: This onshore/offshore split has no US-side equivalent. Because China maintains capital controls, the same currency trades at two prices — a tightly-managed onshore rate (CNY) and a freer offshore rate (CNH) — and the gap between them signals market pressure on the yuan. The US dollar isn't divided into separate onshore and offshore prices, so there's no counterpart structure.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这种在岸/离岸的分割在美国那侧没有对应。由于中国实行资本管制,同一种货币存在两个价格——受较强管理的在岸价(CNY)和更自由的离岸价(CNH)——两者之差反映人民币面临的市场压力。美元并不分为在岸、离岸两个价格,因此没有对应结构。

nodes.definition_en: The institution that manages a country's money and credit — setting interest rates, controlling the money supply, and safeguarding the financial system. China's is the People's Bank of China (PBOC); the US has the Federal Reserve (Fed).
nodes.definition_zh: 管理一国货币与信贷的机构——设定利率、调控货币供应、维护金融体系稳定。中国的是中国人民银行(PBOC),美国的是美联储(Fed)。

nodes.definition_en: When a central bank raises (hike) or lowers (cut) its key interest rates to cool down or stimulate the economy. Higher rates slow borrowing and spending; lower rates encourage them.
nodes.definition_zh: 央行上调(加息)或下调(降息)其关键利率,以给经济降温或提供刺激。利率越高越抑制借贷和支出,越低越鼓励。

nodes.definition_en: The US central bank's main policy interest rate — the rate banks charge each other for overnight loans, which the Fed steers to influence borrowing costs across the whole economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 美国央行的主要政策利率——银行之间隔夜拆借的利率,美联储通过引导它来影响整个经济的借贷成本。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Every central bank has a policy rate, but they're structured differently. The US anchors policy on a single fed funds target. China has no one equivalent benchmark; its policy-rate role is spread across a toolkit — the LPR, the MLF rate, and reverse-repo rates. So when looking for 'China's fed funds rate', there isn't a single number that plays the same role.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 每家央行都有政策利率,但结构不同。美国以单一的联邦基金目标利率为锚。中国没有一个等同的基准;其政策利率角色分散在一套工具中——LPR、MLF 利率、逆回购利率。因此要找「中国版联邦基金利率」时,并没有单一数字扮演相同角色。

nodes.definition_en: The committee within the US Federal Reserve that sets interest-rate policy. It meets on a fixed schedule, votes on rate moves, and publishes a 'dot plot' (点阵图) showing where members expect rates to go.
nodes.definition_zh: 美联储内部制定利率政策的委员会。它按固定日程开会(议息会议),对利率变动投票,并发布展示委员利率预期的「点阵图」(dot plot)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Both central banks make monetary-policy decisions, but the FOMC's machinery is distinctly American. Its scheduled meetings, named voting members, published minutes, and forward-looking dot plot make US policy unusually transparent and calendar-driven. The PBOC's decisions are less tied to a fixed voting-committee schedule and communicated differently, so there's no direct Chinese equivalent to the FOMC as an institution.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 两家央行都做货币政策决策,但 FOMC 的运作机制颇具美国特色。它有固定的会议日程、具名的投票委员、公布的会议纪要以及前瞻性的点阵图,使美国政策异常透明且以日历为节奏。人民银行的决策不那么依赖固定的投票委员会日程、沟通方式也不同,因此作为机构的 FOMC 在中国并无直接对应。

nodes.definition_en: China's benchmark lending rate — the reference rate off which banks price loans. It comes in a 1-year tenor (for most business loans) and a 5-year tenor (which anchors mortgages).
nodes.definition_zh: 中国的贷款基准利率——银行据以为贷款定价的参考利率。它分为1年期(用于多数企业贷款)和5年期(锚定房贷)两档。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The US prime rate is a rough conceptual counterpart — both are benchmark lending rates — but the LPR is set differently. It's quoted by a panel of banks as a spread over the PBOC's MLF rate and guided by policy, and its explicit 1-year/5-year split, with the 5-year steering mortgages, is a distinctive design. So while 'benchmark lending rate' translates, the mechanism and the tenor structure don't map neatly onto the US prime rate.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国的最优惠利率(prime rate)是大致的概念对应——两者都是基准贷款利率——但 LPR 的形成方式不同。它由一组报价行在人民银行 MLF 利率之上加点报出,并受政策引导,且明确分1年期/5年期、由5年期锚定房贷,是独特设计。因此虽然「基准贷款利率」可以对译,其机制与期限结构并不能与美国 prime rate 干净对应。

nodes.zh: 中期借贷便利 / 逆回购
nodes.definition_en: Two tools the People's Bank of China uses to steer money-market conditions and signal policy: the Medium-term Lending Facility (MLF) lends to banks for months at a set rate, and reverse repos inject short-term cash. Their rates act as China's policy rates. MLF is nicknamed 麻辣粉.
nodes.definition_zh: 中国人民银行用来调节货币市场、并释放政策信号的两种工具:中期借贷便利(MLF)以约定利率向银行提供数月期资金,逆回购则投放短期流动性。它们的利率充当中国的政策利率。MLF 俗称「麻辣粉」。
nodes.nuance_note_en: These are China-specific PBOC instruments. The MLF — medium-term central-bank lending to commercial banks at a policy rate — has no US counterpart; the Fed doesn't run an equivalent facility as a core policy lever. While the Fed does conduct reverse repos operationally, they play a floor-setting role rather than serving as a headline policy rate the way the PBOC's reverse-repo rate does. So this toolkit doesn't map onto US monetary operations.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这些是中国人民银行特有的工具。MLF——央行按政策利率向商业银行提供的中期贷款——在美国没有对应;美联储并不把类似工具作为核心政策手段。虽然美联储也进行逆回购操作,但那更多是设定利率下限,而非像人民银行逆回购利率那样充当头条政策利率。因此这套工具无法对应到美国的货币操作。

nodes.definition_en: Lowering the share of deposits that banks must hold in reserve rather than lend out. Cutting it (降准) frees up money for banks to lend, injecting liquidity into the economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 下调银行必须留存、不得放贷的存款比例。降准会释放出可供银行放贷的资金,向经济注入流动性。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The reserve-requirement tool formally exists in both systems, but it's used completely differently. In China, adjusting the RRR (降准) is a frequent, high-profile policy lever the PBOC pulls to manage liquidity. In the US the reserve requirement was cut to zero in 2020 and is no longer an active tool — so the same instrument is a live workhorse in China and effectively dormant in the US.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 存款准备金工具在两套体系里形式上都存在,但用法完全不同。在中国,调整存款准备金率(降准)是人民银行频繁使用、备受瞩目的政策杠杆,用于管理流动性。在美国,准备金率已于2020年降至零,不再是活跃工具——因此同一工具在中国是常用主力,在美国则基本闲置。

nodes.definition_en: Ways a central bank changes the money supply by buying or selling assets. Quantitative easing (QE) creates money to buy bonds and stimulate the economy; quantitative tightening (QT) reverses it, shrinking the central bank's balance sheet.
nodes.definition_zh: 央行通过买卖资产来改变货币供应的方式。量化宽松(QE)创造货币购买债券以刺激经济;缩表(QT)则相反,收缩央行的资产负债表。

nodes.definition_en: Labels for a central banker's leaning. Hawkish means favoring higher rates to fight inflation; dovish means favoring lower rates to support growth and jobs. Markets parse policy statements for which way they tilt.
nodes.definition_zh: 对央行官员立场倾向的标签。鹰派倾向于以更高利率对抗通胀;鸽派倾向于以更低利率支持增长与就业。市场会仔细解读政策措辞偏向哪一方。

nodes.definition_en: A line plotting the interest rates of bonds (usually government bonds) across different maturities. Its shape signals economic expectations; when short-term rates exceed long-term ones, the curve is 'inverted' (倒挂), often read as a recession warning.
nodes.definition_zh: 把不同期限债券(通常是国债)的利率连成的曲线。其形态反映经济预期;当短期利率高于长期利率时,曲线「倒挂」(inversion),常被解读为衰退信号。

nodes.definition_en: The interest rate on a government's 10-year bond — a benchmark that influences borrowing costs and asset prices across the economy.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府十年期债券的利率——一个影响全经济体借贷成本与资产价格的基准。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Same word, different anchor. In English, 'the 10-year Treasury yield' almost always means the US one, widely called the global anchor for pricing assets. In Chinese, 十年期国债收益率 in a domestic context means China's 10-year government bond yield, which is the anchor for domestic pricing. They're two different benchmarks at different levels, so it matters which market's 10-year is being quoted.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 同一说法,所锚不同。英文里「10-year Treasury yield」几乎总指美国的,被广泛称作全球资产定价之锚。中文的「十年期国债收益率」在国内语境下指中国十年期国债收益率,是国内定价之锚。两者是水平不同的两个基准,因此报的是哪个市场的十年期很关键。

nodes.definition_en: In a macro context, how much money and credit is readily available in the economy overall. When a central bank makes money cheap and plentiful, colloquially called 放水 ('releasing water') in Chinese, that's a liquidity injection.
nodes.definition_zh: 在宏观语境下,指整个经济中可随时动用的货币与信贷有多充裕。当央行让资金变得便宜且充沛时,中文口语称为「放水」(liquidity injection)。

nodes.definition_en: A government's use of spending and taxation to steer the economy — spending more or taxing less to boost growth, or the reverse to cool it down. The counterpart to monetary policy, which works through interest rates.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府运用支出和税收来调控经济——多支出或少征税以提振增长,或反向操作以给经济降温。它与通过利率发挥作用的货币政策相对应。

nodes.definition_en: The gap when a government spends more than it collects in revenue over a period, usually expressed relative to GDP as the deficit ratio (赤字率). The shortfall is covered by borrowing.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府在一段时期内支出超过收入的缺口,通常以占GDP的比重表示为赤字率。缺口靠借债弥补。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The concept is the same, but the headline number captures different amounts. China's official deficit ratio (targeted around 3%) covers only the general public budget and leaves out large volumes of off-budget borrowing — notably local-government special bonds — so the official figure understates the true fiscal stance. US deficit measures fold in more of the government's borrowing, so the two headline deficits aren't measuring the same thing.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 概念相同,但头条数字涵盖的范围不同。中国的官方赤字率(目标约3%)只覆盖一般公共预算,把大量预算外举债——尤其是地方政府专项债——排除在外,因此官方数字低估了真实的财政力度。美国的赤字口径纳入更多政府举债,故两地的头条赤字并非同一口径。

nodes.definition_en: A bond issued by Chinese local governments to fund specific public projects that are expected to earn their own income (such as a toll road), repaid out of that project's revenue rather than from general tax money.
nodes.definition_zh: 中国地方政府为特定、预期能自身产生收益的公共项目(如收费公路)而发行的债券,以该项目的收入而非一般税收来偿还。
nodes.nuance_note_en: A China-specific fiscal tool with no clean US match. 专项债 are a designated portion of local-government borrowing tied to revenue-generating projects, and China's budget rules count them separately from ordinary 'general' government bonds. US 'revenue bonds' share the broad idea of project-linked repayment, but 专项债 live inside a particular Chinese quota-and-policy framework with no direct equivalent, so the two shouldn't be treated as interchangeable.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 中国特有的财政工具,美国没有干净的对应物。专项债是地方政府举债中专门与有收益项目挂钩的一类,在中国的预算制度下与普通「一般债」分开核算。美国的 revenue bond 虽同样有「以项目收入偿还」的思路,但专项债处于中国特定的额度与政策框架之内,并无直接对等物,故两者不宜简单互换。

nodes.definition_en: In China, a company set up by a local government to borrow money and fund public projects like roads and infrastructure, keeping that debt off the government's own books. 'LGFV' refers both to these entities and, loosely, to the bonds they issue.
nodes.definition_zh: 在中国,由地方政府设立、用于举债并为道路及基础设施等公共项目融资的公司,使相关债务不直接体现在政府自身账面上。「城投」既指这类平台,也泛指它们发行的债券。
nodes.nuance_note_en: No US counterpart. LGFVs exist because Chinese local governments have historically faced limits on borrowing directly, so they created arm's-length companies to raise the money — producing a category of debt that sits in a grey zone between corporate risk and government risk. US municipal bonds are issued by the government body itself and carry no comparable ambiguity, so nothing maps directly onto the 城投 concept.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 美国无对应物。城投之所以存在,是因为中国地方政府过去直接举债受到限制,于是设立相对独立的平台公司来融资——由此形成一类介于企业信用与政府信用之间的灰色债务。美国市政债由政府主体自身发行,不存在类似的模糊性,因而没有与「城投」直接对应的概念。

nodes.definition_en: Government measures — extra spending, tax cuts, or easier credit — meant to jolt a slowing economy back to growth. In China these are often framed with phrases like '一揽子增量政策' (a package of incremental policies).
nodes.definition_zh: 政府为让放缓的经济重回增长而采取的措施——增加支出、减税或放松信贷。在中国,常以「一揽子增量政策」等表述呈现。

nodes.definition_en: A tax a government puts on imported goods, making foreign products more expensive to protect domestic industry or as a lever in trade disputes. A high-frequency term in the US–China trade context.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府对进口商品征收的税,使外国产品变贵,用以保护本国产业或作为贸易争端中的筹码。在中美贸易语境下是高频词。

nodes.definition_en: Government restrictions that penalize a country, company, or individual. An 'entity list' names specific firms that domestic companies are barred from doing certain business with — a common risk for technology stocks.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府用于惩罚某国、某公司或某人的限制措施。「实体清单」列出特定企业,禁止本国公司与其开展某些业务——是科技股常见的风险。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Sanctions are a universal tool, but 'entity list' points to a specific US mechanism — the US Commerce Department's Entity List, which restricts exports to named firms and has been a major risk for Chinese tech companies. China has since built its own mirror mechanism (the Unreliable Entity List, 不可靠实体清单). So the instruments now exist on both sides but are separate, asymmetric lists rather than a single shared concept.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 制裁是通用工具,但「实体清单」指向一个特定的美国机制——美国商务部的实体清单,限制向被列名企业出口,曾是中国科技公司的重大风险。中国其后也建立了对应机制(不可靠实体清单)。因此如今两侧都有相应工具,但它们是彼此独立、并不对称的清单,而非单一共享概念。

nodes.definition_en: A government's deliberate effort to shape which industries grow — through subsidies, targets, and support for favored sectors. In China it's a central economic lever, framed with terms like 新质生产力 (new quality productive forces) and 国产替代 (domestic substitution).
nodes.definition_zh: 政府有意引导哪些产业发展的努力——通过补贴、目标和对重点行业的扶持。在中国这是核心经济杠杆,常以「新质生产力」「国产替代」(domestic substitution)等表述呈现。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Industrial policy exists in many countries — the US has recently leaned on it with measures like the CHIPS Act — but its scale and centrality in China are far greater, and it comes with a distinctive vocabulary and long-term planning apparatus. So while the concept translates, a US reader should not assume the light-touch American version is comparable to how pervasively industrial policy steers the Chinese economy.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 产业政策在许多国家都有——美国近年也借助《芯片法案》等措施加以运用——但其在中国的规模和核心地位要大得多,并伴随独特的话语体系和长期规划机制。因此概念虽可对译,美国读者不应以为美式的较轻介入,能与产业政策在中国经济中那种全面主导的程度相提并论。

nodes.definition_en: A company owned and controlled by the government rather than private shareholders. In China these split into central SOEs (央企) and local SOEs (地方国企), and carry policy themes like SOE reform and 中特估 (a re-rating of SOE valuations).
nodes.definition_zh: 由政府而非私人股东拥有和控制的公司。在中国分为央企和地方国企,并牵涉国企改革、中特估(SOE valuation re-rating)等政策题材。
nodes.nuance_note_en: State-owned enterprises exist in many economies, but their role in China is vastly larger — they dominate strategic sectors and are central to policy in a way that has no US parallel (the US has only a few, like the Postal Service or Amtrak). China also generates distinctive market themes around them, such as 中特估, the idea of re-rating SOE valuations to reflect their special status — a concept with no US counterpart.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 国有企业在许多经济体都有,但在中国的角色要大得多——它们主导战略行业,在政策中处于核心地位,这一点在美国并无对应(美国仅有邮政、美铁等少数几家)。中国还围绕国企产生了独特的市场题材,如中特估,即重估国企估值以反映其特殊地位——这是美国没有对应的概念。

nodes.definition_en: A government body that supervises and sets rules for the financial system. China's include the securities regulator (CSRC), the central bank (PBOC), and the financial-supervision administration (NFRA); the US has the SEC, Fed, and CFTC, among others.
nodes.definition_zh: 监督金融体系并制定规则的政府机构。中国的包括证监会(CSRC)、央行(PBOC)、金融监管总局(NFRA);美国有证券交易委员会(SEC)、美联储(Fed)、商品期货交易委员会(CFTC)等。

nodes.definition_en: A way of approving IPOs based on full disclosure rather than official gatekeeping: as long as a company discloses properly, the market decides whether to buy, instead of a regulator judging its merits. China has been shifting to this (注册制) from an earlier approval-based system (核准制).
nodes.definition_zh: 一种以充分信息披露、而非官方审批把关来批准IPO的方式:只要公司如实披露,由市场决定是否买入,而非由监管评判其优劣。中国正从此前的核准制转向注册制。
nodes.nuance_note_en: The underlying function — how new shares get cleared to list — exists in both markets, but the histories differ. The US has long been disclosure/registration-based, so this is simply the default there. China is mid-transition: it historically used an approval-based system (核准制) where a regulator gated who could list and shaped pricing, and 注册制 is the reform moving it closer to the US model. So the term marks a reform milestone in China that has no equivalent narrative in the US.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 底层功能——新股如何获准上市——两地都有,但历史不同。美国长期实行披露/注册制,因此这在那里只是默认状态。中国正处于转型之中:过去采用核准制,由监管把关谁能上市并影响定价,注册制则是使其向美式靠拢的改革。因此该词在中国标志着一个改革里程碑,在美国并无对应的叙事。

nodes.definition_en: Government restrictions on how money can move in and out of a country. They limit residents and foreigners from freely converting currency or moving capital across the border.
nodes.definition_zh: 政府对资金跨境流入流出方式的限制,约束居民和外国人自由兑换货币或跨境转移资本。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Capital controls are a recognized tool, and the US essentially doesn't use them — money moves freely across its border. China does use them extensively: the yuan's capital account isn't fully open, and cross-border investing runs through quota systems like QFII (for foreign money coming in) and QDII (for domestic money going out). So the same concept is largely absent in the US but a pervasive feature of the Chinese system.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 资本管制是公认的工具,美国基本不使用——资金可自由跨境流动。中国则广泛使用:人民币资本项目未完全开放,跨境投资通过 QFII(外资流入)、QDII(内资流出)等额度制度进行。因此同一概念在美国基本缺席,却是中国体系中普遍存在的特征。

nodes.definition_en: Two policy themes used in analyzing Chinese regulatory risk. Anti-monopoly is action against companies that dominate a market unfairly; common prosperity (共同富裕) is a Chinese policy goal of narrowing inequality and spreading the gains of growth more evenly.
nodes.definition_zh: 分析中国监管风险时常用的两个政策主题。反垄断是针对不正当垄断市场的企业采取的行动;共同富裕(common prosperity)是中国缩小贫富差距、让增长成果更均衡分配的政策目标。
nodes.nuance_note_en: These two split cleanly. Anti-monopoly has a direct US counterpart in antitrust law and enforcement. Common prosperity does not — it's a distinctly Chinese policy frame with no US equivalent, and as a signal it can move markets by hinting at redistribution or pressure on high-earning sectors. So half of this pair translates neatly and half requires explaining a China-specific agenda.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 这两者可以清楚地分开。反垄断在美国有直接对应,即反垄断法及其执法。共同富裕则没有——它是中国特有的政策框架,美国无对应,作为信号,它可能因暗示再分配或对高收入行业施压而牵动市场。因此这对词一半可以顺畅对译,另一半则需解释一项中国特有的政策议程。

nodes.definition_en: Government measures aimed at the property market — cooling or supporting home prices and developer activity. In China these include purchase restrictions (限购) and the 'three red lines' (三条红线) that cap how much developers can borrow.
nodes.definition_zh: 针对房地产市场的政府措施——为房价和开发商活动降温或提供支持。在中国,这些措施包括限购,以及限制开发商举债的「三条红线」(three red lines)。
nodes.nuance_note_en: Every country has housing policy, so the general concept has a counterpart, but China's specific toolkit does not. Instruments like 限购 (barring people from buying additional homes) and 三条红线 (hard leverage limits on developers) are administrative levers with no direct US equivalent; US housing policy works more through interest rates, tax treatment, and government-backed mortgage agencies. So the category translates but the tools don't.
nodes.nuance_note_zh: 每个国家都有住房政策,因此总体概念有对应,但中国特有的工具箱没有。像限购(禁止购买额外住房)、三条红线(对开发商的硬性杠杆限制)这类行政杠杆,美国没有直接对应;美国的住房政策更多通过利率、税收待遇和政府支持的房贷机构发挥作用。因此类别可对译,工具则不能。

nodes.definition_en: Metaphors for how an overheating economy slows down. A soft landing is a gentle cooling that avoids recession; a hard landing is an abrupt slowdown that tips into a downturn.
nodes.definition_zh: 对过热经济如何减速的比喻。软着陆是温和降温、避免衰退;硬着陆是骤然放缓、跌入下行。

nodes.definition_en: A significant, sustained decline in economic activity, commonly marked by falling output and rising unemployment. When a downturn combines with high inflation, it's called stagflation (滞胀).
nodes.definition_zh: 经济活动显著且持续的下滑,通常表现为产出下降、失业上升。当下行与高通胀并存时,称为滞胀(stagflation)。
